pages.title: Topologies of Grothendieck
pages.html:
1 Topologies of Grothendieck

Originally, Grothendieck's topologies appeared as underlying his theory of descent (cf. SGA 1 VI, VIII); the use of the corresponding cohomology theory is later. The same approach is followed here: by formalizing the classical notions of localization, local ownership and recollation (1.1, 1.2, 1.3), the general concept of Grothendieck's topology (1.6); to justify the introduction into algebraic geometry, a fairly flat descent theorem is shown (1.4), generalization of Hilbert's classical theorem (1.5).

The reader will find a more complete but concise exhibition of formalism in Giraud [19]. Mr Artin's notes: "Grothendieck topologies" [1] (Chapters I to III) are also useful. The 866 pages of the I to V presentations of SGA 4 are valuable when looking at exotic topologies, such as the one that gives rise to crystal cohomology; to use the lethal topology so close to classical intuition, it is not indispensable to read them.

1.1. Targets

Be \(X\) a topological space and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{R}\) a real value function on \(X\). The continuity of \(f\) is a local property; in other words, if \(f\) is continuous on all open sufficiently small of \(X\), \(f\) is continuous on \(X\) as a whole. To formalize the notion of "local property," we will introduce some definitions.

It is said that a set \(\mathscr{U}\) of open \(X\) is a sifting if for everything \(U\in\mathscr{U}\) and \(V\subset U\), we have \(V\in\mathscr{U}\). It is said that a screen is covering if the meeting of all the openings belonging to this screen is equal to \(X\).

Given a family \(\{U_i\}\) of open \(X\), the screen generated by \(\{U_i\}\) is by definition all open \(U\) of \(X\) such that \(U\) is contained in one of \(U_i\).

It is said that a property \(P(U)\), defined for all open \(U\) of \(X\), is local if, for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) of any open \(U\) of \(X\), \(P(U)\) is true if and only if \(P(V)\) is true for all \(V\in \mathscr{U}\). For example, given \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{R}\), the property "\(f\) is continuous on \(U\)" is local.

1.2 sheaves

Let's specify the concept of function given locally on \(X\).

1.2.1 From the point of view of sifting

Either \(\mathscr{U}\) an open screen of \(X\). We call given function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally on \(X\) the data for all \(U\in \mathscr{U}\) of a function \(f_U\) on \(U\) such that if \(V\subset U\) we have \(f_V=f_U|V\).

1.2.2 Point of view of Čech

If the \(\mathscr{U}\) is generated by an open family \(U_i\) of \(X\), giving oneself a function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself a function \(f_i\) on each \(U_i\), such as \(f_i|{U_i\cap U_j} = f_j|{U_i\cap U_j}\).

In other words, if \(Z=\coprod U_i\), giving oneself a function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself a function on \(Z\) that is constant on the fibers of the natural projection \(Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\).

1.2.3

The continuous functions form a sheaf; this means that for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) of an open \(V\) of \(X\) and any given function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally \(\{f_U\}\) such that each \(f_U\) is continuous on \(U\), there is a single continuous function \(f\) on \(V\) such as \(f|U=f_U\) for all \(U\in \mathscr{U}\).

Fields

Let us now specify the concept of vector fibration given locally on \(X\).

1.3.1 Perspective of sifting

Either \(\mathscr{U}\) an open screen of \(X\). It is called given vector fibrous \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally on \(X\) the data of

  1. a vector fibre \(E_U\) on each \(U\in \mathscr{U}\),

  2. if \(V\subset U\), an isomorphism \(\rho_{U,V} : E_V\xrightarrow{\sim} E_U|V\), verifying

  3. if \(W\subset V\subset U\), the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ E_W \ar[r]^-{\rho_{U,W}} \ar[dr]_-{\rho_{V,W}} & E_U|W \\ & E_V|W \ar[u]_-{\rho_{U,V}|W} } \]

    switch, i.e. \(\rho_{U,V} = (\rho_{U,V} \mbox{ restreint à } W) \circ \rho_{V,W}\).

1.3.2 Čech perspective

If the screen \(\mathscr{U}\) is generated by a family of open \(U_i\) of \(X\), giving oneself a vector fibrous \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself:

  1. a vector fibre \(E_i\) on each \(U_i\),

  2. if \(U_{ij} = U_i\cap U_j = U_i\times_X U_j\), isomorphism \(\rho_{ji} : E_i|U_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} E_j|U_{ij}\), so that

  3. if \(U_{ijk} = U_i\times_X U_j\times_X U_k\), the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ E_i |U_{ijk} \ar[r]^-{\rho_{ki}|U_{ijk}} \ar[dr]_-{\rho_{ji}|U_{ijk}} & E_k|U_{ijk} \\ & E_j|U_{ijk} \ar[u]_-{\rho_{kj}|U_{ijk}} } \]

    switch, i.e. \(\rho_{ki} = \rho_{kj}\circ \rho_{ji}\) to \(U_{ijk}\).

In other words, if \(Z=\coprod U_i\) and if \(\pi:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the natural projection, giving oneself a vector fibrous \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself:

  1. a vector fibre \(E\) on \(Z\),

  2. if \(x\) and \(y\) are two points of \(Z\) such as \(\pi(x) = \pi(y)\), an isomorphism \(\rho_{y x}:E_x\xrightarrow{\sim} E_y\) between the fibres of \(E\) in \(x\) and \(y\), continuously dependent on \((x,y)\) and such,

  3. if \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\) are three points of \(Z\) such as \(\pi(x)=\pi(y)=\pi(z)\), we have \(\rho_{zx} = \rho_{zy}\circ \rho_{yx}\).

1.3.3

A vector fibre \(E\) on \(X\) defines a given vector fibre \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally \(E_\mathscr{U}\); the restriction system \(E_U\) from \(E\) to the objects of \(\mathscr{U}\). The fact that the vector fibre concept is local in nature can be expressed as follows: for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) from \(X\), the searcher \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{U}\), vector fibres on \(X\) in the given vector fibres \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally, is an equivalence of categories.

1.3.4

If in 1 we replace "open from \(X\)" with "part of \(X\)," we get the notion of the subspace sifting of \(X\). In this context also we have recollage theorems. For example: be \(X\) a space and \(\mathscr{C}\) a sifting of subspaces of \(X\) generated by a locally finished closed overlay of \(X\), then the \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{C}\), vector fibres on \(X\) in given vector fibres \(\mathscr{C}\)-locally is an equivalence of categories.

In algebraic geometry, it is useful to also consider "space cracks above \(X\)"; this is what we will see in the next paragraph.

1.4 Accurately flat descent

1.4.1

In the framework of the diagrams, the topology of Zariski is not fine enough for the study of nonlinear problems and it is necessary to replace in the previous definitions the immersions opened by more general morphisms. From this point of view, the techniques of descent appear as techniques of localization. Thus the following statement of descent can express by saying that the properties considered are of a local nature for the faithfully flat topology (it is said that a morphism of diagrams is faithfully flat if it is flat is overjective).

Proposition 1.4.2

Be \(A\) a ring and \(B\) a \(A\)-algebra faithfully flat. So:

  1. A continuation \(\Sigma=(M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'')\) of \(A\)-modules is correct as soon as the continuation \(\Sigma_{(B)}\) which is deduced by extension of scalar to \(B\) is correct.

  2. A \(A\)-module \(M\) is of finite type (finite presentation, flat, locally free of finished row, invertible (i.e. locally free of rank one)) as soon as \(B\)-module \(M_{(B)}\) is.

Proof▾

(i) The \(M\mapsto M_{(B)}\) being accurate (plitude of \(B\)), it is sufficient to show that, if a \(A\)-module \(N\) is non-zero, \(N_{(B)}\) is non-zero. If \(N\) is non-zero, \(N\) contains a non-zero monogenous submodule \(A/\mathfrak{a}\); then \(N_{(B)}\) contains a monogenous submodule \((A/\mathfrak{a})_{(B)} = B/\mathfrak{a} B\), not null by overemitting structural morphism \(\varphi:\operatorname{Spec}(B)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) (if \(V(\mathfrak{a})\) is not empty, \(\varphi^{-1}(V(\mathfrak{a}))=V(\mathfrak{a} B)\) is not empty).

(ii) For any family \((x_i)\) of \(M_{(B)}\), there is a finite type submodule \(M'\) of \(M\) such that \(M'_{(B)}\) contains \(x_i\). If \(M_{(B)}\) is finite type and \(x_i\) generate \(M_{(B)}\), there is \(M'_{(B)}=M_{(B)}\), therefore \(M'=M\) and \(M\) is finite type.

C.Q.F.D.

If \(M_{(B)}\) is of finite presentation, one can, after the above, find an overjection \(A^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\). If \(N\) is the kernel of this overjection, the \(B\)-module \(N_{(B)}\) is of finite type, therefore \(N\) is of finite presentation, and \(M\) is of finite presentation. The assertion for "flat" is immediately from (i); "finite row free" means "flat and finite presentation" and the row is tested by extension of scalar to fields.

1.4.3

Be \(X\) a scheme and \(\mathscr{S}\) a class of \(X\)-schémas stable per fibrated product on \(X\). A class \(\mathscr{U}\subset \mathscr{S}\) is a sifting on \(X\) (relative to \(\mathscr{S}\)) if, for any morphism \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) of \(X\)-schemes, with \(U,V\in \mathscr{S}\) and \(U\in\mathscr{U}\), we have \(V\in \mathscr{U}\). The screen generated by a family \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\)-schemes in \(\mathscr{S}\) is the class of \(V\in\mathscr{S}\) such that there is a morphism of \(X\)-schemes of \(V\) in one of \(U_i\).

1.4.4

Either \(\mathscr{U}\) a screen on \(X\). The quasi-coherent module given \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally on \(X\) the data of

  1. a quasi-coherent module \(E_U\) on each \(U\in\mathscr{U}\),

  2. for everything \(U\in\mathscr{U}\) and for any morphism \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) of \(X\)-schemes in \(\mathscr{S}\), an isomorphism \(\rho_\varphi:E_V\xrightarrow{\sim} \varphi^* E_U\), these being such that

  3. if \(\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) is a morphism of \(X\)-schemes in \(\mathscr{S}\), the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ E_W \ar[r]^-{\rho_{\varphi\circ\psi}} \ar[dr]_-{\rho_\psi} & \psi^*\varphi^* E_U \\ & \psi^* E_V \ar[u]_-{\psi^*\rho_\varphi} } \]

    switch, i.e. \(\rho_{\varphi\circ\psi}=(\psi^*\rho_\varphi)\circ \rho_\psi\).

If \(E\) is a quasi-coherent module on \(X\), we note \(E_\mathscr{U}\) the given module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally valid \(\varphi_U^* E\) on \(\varphi:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and such that, for any morphism \(\psi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) the restriction isomorphism \(\rho_\psi\) is canonical isomorphism \(E_V=(\varphi_U\circ \psi)^* E\xrightarrow{\sim} \psi^* \varphi_U^* E = \psi^* E_U\).

Theorem 1.4.5

Either \(\{U_i\}\in\mathscr{S}\) a finished family of \(X\)-flats on \(X\) such that \(X\) either the reuniting of images of \(U_i\), and either \(\mathscr{U}\) the screen generated by \(\{U_i\}\). Then the \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{U}\) is an equivalence of the category of quasi-coherent modules on \(X\) with the category of quasi-coherent modules given \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally.

Proof▾

We will only deal with the case where \(x\) is affine and \(\mathscr{U}\) is generated by \(X\)-scheme affine \(U\) faithfully flat on \(X\). The reduction in this case is formal. \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\).

If the morphism \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) admits a section, \(X\) belongs to the screen \(\mathscr{U}\) and the assertion is obvious. We will reduce ourselves to this case.

A given quasi-coherent module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally defines modules \(M'\), \(M''\) and \(M'''\) on \(U\), \(U\times_X U\) and \(U\times_X U\times_X U\), and isomorphisms \(\rho:p^* M^\bullet\simeq M^\bullet\) for any projection morphism \(p\) between these spaces; this is a Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ M^* : M' \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M'' \ar[r] \ar@<4pt>[r] \ar@<-4pt>[r] & M''' } \]

above

\[ \xymatrix{ U_* : U & U\times_X U \ar@<2pt>[l] \ar@<-2pt>[l] & U\times_X U\times_X U \ar[l] \ar@<4pt>[l] \ar@<-4pt>[l] . } \]

Reciprocally \(M^*\) determines the given module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally: for \(V\in\mathscr{U}\), there is \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) and we pose \(M_U=\varphi^* M'\); for \(\varphi_1,\varphi_2:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\), we have a natural identification \(\varphi_1^* M'\simeq (\varphi_1\times \varphi_2)^* M'' \simeq \varphi_2^* M'\), and we see using \(M'''\) u these identifications are compatible, so the definition is legitimate. In short, it is up to the same to give each other a module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally or a diagram \(M^*\) Cartesian on \(U_*\).

Translations in algebraic terms: giving oneself \(M^*\) amounts to giving oneself a Cartesian diagram of modules

\[ \xymatrix{ M' \ar@<3pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar@<-3pt>[r]|-{\partial_1} & M'' \ar@<6pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar[r]|-{\partial_1} \ar@<-6pt>[r]|-{\partial_2} & M''' } \]

above the ring diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ B \ar@<3pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar@<-3pt>[r]|-{\partial_1} & B\otimes_A B \ar@<6pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar[r]|-{\partial_1} \ar@<-6pt>[r]|-{\partial_2} & B\otimes_A B\otimes_A B } \]

(species: we have \(\partial_i(b m)=\partial_i(b)\cdot\partial_i(m)\), the usual identities such as \(\partial_0\partial_1=\partial_0\partial_0\) are true, and "cardesian" means that the morphisms \(\partial_i:M'\otimes_{B,\partial_i}(B\otimes_A B)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\) and \(M''\otimes_{B\otimes_A B,\partial_i}(B\otimes_A B\otimes_A B)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'''\) are isomorphisms).

The actuator \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{U}\) becomes the actuator that, to a \(A\)-module \(M\), associates

\[ \xymatrix{ M^* = (M\otimes_A B \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M\otimes_A B\otimes_A B \ar@<-4pt>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<4pt>[r] & M\otimes_A B\otimes_A B\otimes_A B). } \]

He admits as deputy to the right the striker

\[ \xymatrix{ (M' \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M'' \ar@<-4pt>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<4pt>[r] & M''') \ar@{|->}[r] & \ker(M' \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M'''). } \]

We have to prove that the addition arrows

\[ M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\ker(M\otimes_A B\rightrightarrows M\otimes_A B\otimes_A B) \]

and

\[ \ker(M'\rightrightarrows M'')\otimes_A B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M' \]

are isomorphisms.1.4.2.i), it is sufficient to prove it after a change of base faithfully flat \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\) (\(B\) becoming \(B'=B\otimes_A A'\)). Taking \(A'=B\), this brings us back to the case where \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) admits a section.

C.Q.F.D.

1.5 A special case: Hilbert's Theorem 90

1.5.1

Be \(k\) a field, \(k'\) a Galoisian extension of \(k\) and \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(k'/k)\). So homo~morphism

\begin{align*} k'\otimes_k k' &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \oplus_{\sigma\in G} k' \\ x\otimes y &\mapsto \{x\cdot \sigma(y)\}_{\sigma\in G} \end{align*}

is bijective.

It is deduced that it is the same to give oneself a module locally for the screen generated by \(\operatorname{Spec}(k')\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) or to give oneself a \(k'\)-vectorial space with a semilinear action of \(G\), i.e.:

  1. a \(k'\)-vectorial space \(V'\),

  2. for all \(\sigma\in G\), an endomorphism \(\varphi_\sigma\) of the group structure of \(V'\) such as \(\varphi_\sigma(\lambda v) = \sigma(\lambda) \varphi_\sigma(v)\), for all \(\lambda\in k'\) and \(v\in V'\), checking the condition

  3. for everything \(\sigma,\tau\in G\), we have \(\varphi_{\tau\sigma} = \varphi_\tau\circ\varphi_\sigma\).

Either \(V={V'}^G\) the group of invariants by this action of \(G\); it is a \(k\)-vectorial space and, according to the theorem 1.4.5, we have:

Proposition 1.5.2

The inclusion of \(V\) in \(V'\) defines an isomorphism \(V\otimes_k k' \xrightarrow{\sim} V'\).

In particular, if \(V'\) is of size \(1\) and \(v'\in V\) is not null, \(\varphi_\sigma\) is determined by the constant \(c(\sigma)\in {k'}^\times\) such that \(\varphi_\sigma(v')=c(\sigma)v'\) and condition (c) is written

\[ c(\tau\sigma) = c(\tau)\cdot \tau(c(\sigma))\text{.} \]

According to the proposal there is an invariant vector that is not harmful \(v=\mu v'\), \(\mu\in {k'}^\times\). Therefore we have for everything \(\sigma\in G\),

\[ c(\sigma) = \mu\cdot \sigma(\mu^{-1})\text{.} \]

In other words, everything \(1\)-cocycle of \(G\) to values in \({k'}^\times\) is a starboard:

Corollary 1.5.3

We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(G,{k'}^\times)=0\).

1.6 Topologies of Grothendieck

We now transcribe the definitions of the preceding paragraphs into an abstract framework encompassing both the case of topological spaces and that of schematics.

1.6.1

Be \(\mathscr{S}\) a category and \(U\) an object of \(\mathscr{S}\). sifting on \(U\) a subset \(\mathscr{U}\) of \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{S}/U)\) such that \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belongs to \(\mathscr{U}\) and if \(\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) is a morphism in \(\mathscr{S}\), then \(\varphi\circ\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belongs to \(\mathscr{U}\).

If \(\{\varphi_i:U_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\}\) is a family of morphisms, the screen generated by \(U_i\) is by definition the set of morphisms \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) that are factored through one of \(\varphi_i\).

If \(\mathscr{U}\) is a sifter on \(U\) and if \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) is a morphism, the restriction \(\mathscr{U}_V\) from \(\mathscr{U}\) to \(V\) is by definition the sifter on \(V\) constituted or morphisms \(\psi:w\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) such as \(\varphi\circ\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belongs to \(\mathscr{U}\).

1.6.2

The data of a topology of Grothendieck on \(\mathscr{S}\) consists of the data for any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\) of a set \(C(U)\) of sifting on \(U\), said sifting coverings, so that the following axioms are satisfied:

  1. The screen generated by the identity of \(U\) is covered.

  2. If \(\mathscr{U}\) is a screen covering \(U\) and \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) is a morphism, the screen \(\mathscr{U}_V\) is covered.

  3. A locally covered screen is covered. In other words, if \(\mathscr{U}\) is a screen covering \(U\) and if \(\mathscr{U}'\) is a screen on \(U\) such that, for all \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belonging to \(\mathscr{U}\), the screen \(\mathscr{U}_V'\) is covered, then \(\mathscr{U}'\) is covered.

We're calling. site data from a category with Grothendieck topology.

1.6.3

Given a site \(\mathscr{S}\), it is called prefaisceau on \(\mathscr{S}\) a contravariant actuator \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(\mathscr{S}\) in the category of sets. For any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\), it is called section of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(U\) the elements of \(\mathscr{F}(U)\). For any morphism \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) and for all \(s\in \mathscr{F}(U)\), it is noted \(s|V\) (\(s\) restricted to \(V\)) the image of \(s\) in \(\mathscr{F}(V)\).

If \(\mathscr{U}\) is a screen on \(U\), we call the given section \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally the data, for all \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belonging to \(\mathscr{U}\), of a section \(s_V\in\mathscr{F}(V)\) such that, for any morphism \(W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\), we sit \(s_V|W=s_W\). It is said that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a sheaf if, for any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\), for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) on \(U\) and for any given section \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally \(\{s_V\}\), there is a single section \(s\in\mathscr{F}(U)\) such as \(s|V=s_V\), for all \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belonging to \(\mathscr{U}\).

A similar definition is given of the abelian sheaves It is shown that the category of abelian sheaves on \(\mathscr{S}\) is an abelian category with sufficient injections. A sequence \(\mathscr{F}\xrightarrow f \mathscr{G}\xrightarrow g \mathscr{H}\) of sheaves is correct if, for any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\), and for all \(s\in \mathscr{G}(U)\) such as \(g(s)=0\), there exists locally \(t\) such as \(f(t)=s\); i.e. if there is a screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) on \(U\) and for all \(V\in\mathscr{U}\), a section \(t_V\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(V\) such as \(f(t_V)=s|V\).

1.6.4 Examples

We saw two higher.

  1. Be \(X\) a topological space and \(\mathscr{S}\) the category whose objects are the openings of \(X\) and morphisms natural inclusions. The topological of Grothendieck on \(\mathscr{S}\) corresponding to the usual topology of \(X\) is that for which a screen \(\mathscr{U}\) on an open \(U\) of \(X\) is covering if the meeting of the openings belonging to this screen is equal to \(U\). It is clear that the category of sheaves on \(\mathscr{S}\) is equivalent to the category of sheaves on \(X\) in the usual sense.

  2. Are \(X\) a scheme and \(\mathscr{S}\) the category of schemes on \(X\). We call topology fpqc (almost compact flat fidelity) on \(\mathscr{S}\) the topology of Grothendieck for which a screen on a \(X\)-scheme \(U\) is covering if it is generated by a finite family of flat morphisms whose images cover \(U\).

1.6.5 Cohomology

It will always be assumed that the category \(\mathscr{S}\) has a final object \(X\). Then we call the global sections of an abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\), and we note \(\Gamma\mathscr{F}\) or \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{F})\), the group \(\mathscr{F}(X)\). The \(\mathscr{F}\mapsto\Gamma\mathscr{F}\) is an exact enteror to the left of the category of abelian sheaves on \(\mathscr{S}\) in the category of abelian groups, or note \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,-)\) its derivatives (or satellites). These cohomology groups represent obstructions to move from local to global. By definition, if \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{H}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) is an exact sequence of abelian sheaves, we have an exact long sequence of cohomology:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{H}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \cdots \\ \ldots \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(X,\mathscr{H}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{n+1}(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

1.6.6

Given an abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(\mathscr{S}\), it is called \(\mathscr{F}\)-torsor a sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) equipped with an action \(\mathscr{F}\times\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such as locally (after restriction to all objects of a screen covering the final object \(X\)) \(\mathscr{G}\) equipped with the action of \(\mathscr{F}\) or isomorph to \(\mathscr{F}\) equipped with the canonical action \(\mathscr{F}\times \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) by translations.

It can be shown that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is interpreted as all classes with isomorphism near \(\mathscr{F}\)-torsors.

blocks.html: 1 Topologies of Grothendieck

Originally, Grothendieck's topologies appeared as underlying his theory of descent (cf. SGA 1 VI, VIII); the use of the corresponding cohomology theory is later. The same approach is followed here: by formalizing the classical notions of localization, local ownership and recollation (1.1, 1.2, 1.3), the general concept of Grothendieck's topology (1.6); to justify the introduction into algebraic geometry, a fairly flat descent theorem is shown (1.4), generalization of Hilbert's classical theorem (1.5).

The reader will find a more complete but concise exhibition of formalism in Giraud [19]. Mr Artin's notes: "Grothendieck topologies" [1] (Chapters I to III) are also useful. The 866 pages of the I to V presentations of SGA 4 are valuable when looking at exotic topologies, such as the one that gives rise to crystal cohomology; to use the lethal topology so close to classical intuition, it is not indispensable to read them.
1.1. Targets

Be \(X\) a topological space and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{R}\) a real value function on \(X\). The continuity of \(f\) is a local property; in other words, if \(f\) is continuous on all open sufficiently small of \(X\), \(f\) is continuous on \(X\) as a whole. To formalize the notion of "local property," we will introduce some definitions.

It is said that a set \(\mathscr{U}\) of open \(X\) is a sifting if for everything \(U\in\mathscr{U}\) and \(V\subset U\), we have \(V\in\mathscr{U}\). It is said that a screen is covering if the meeting of all the openings belonging to this screen is equal to \(X\).
Given a family \(\{U_i\}\) of open \(X\), the screen generated by \(\{U_i\}\) is by definition all open \(U\) of \(X\) such that \(U\) is contained in one of \(U_i\).

It is said that a property \(P(U)\), defined for all open \(U\) of \(X\), is local if, for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) of any open \(U\) of \(X\), \(P(U)\) is true if and only if \(P(V)\) is true for all \(V\in \mathscr{U}\). For example, given \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{R}\), the property "\(f\) is continuous on \(U\)" is local.
1.2 sheaves
Let's specify the concept of function given locally on \(X\).
1.2.1 From the point of view of sifting

Either \(\mathscr{U}\) an open screen of \(X\). We call given function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally on \(X\) the data for all \(U\in \mathscr{U}\) of a function \(f_U\) on \(U\) such that if \(V\subset U\) we have \(f_V=f_U|V\).
1.2.2 Point of view of Čech

If the \(\mathscr{U}\) is generated by an open family \(U_i\) of \(X\), giving oneself a function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself a function \(f_i\) on each \(U_i\), such as \(f_i|{U_i\cap U_j} = f_j|{U_i\cap U_j}\).

In other words, if \(Z=\coprod U_i\), giving oneself a function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself a function on \(Z\) that is constant on the fibers of the natural projection \(Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\).

The continuous functions form a sheaf; this means that for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) of an open \(V\) of \(X\) and any given function \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally \(\{f_U\}\) such that each \(f_U\) is continuous on \(U\), there is a single continuous function \(f\) on \(V\) such as \(f|U=f_U\) for all \(U\in \mathscr{U}\).
Fields
Let us now specify the concept of vector fibration given locally on \(X\).
1.3.1 Perspective of sifting
Either \(\mathscr{U}\) an open screen of \(X\). It is called given vector fibrous \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally on \(X\) the data of

1. a vector fibre \(E_U\) on each \(U\in \mathscr{U}\),

  2. if \(V\subset U\), an isomorphism \(\rho_{U,V} : E_V\xrightarrow{\sim} E_U|V\), verifying

  3. if \(W\subset V\subset U\), the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ E_W \ar[r]^-{\rho_{U,W}} \ar[dr]_-{\rho_{V,W}} & E_U|W \\ & E_V|W \ar[u]_-{\rho_{U,V}|W} } \]

    switch, i.e. \(\rho_{U,V} = (\rho_{U,V} \mbox{ restreint à } W) \circ \rho_{V,W}\).
1.3.2 Čech perspective
If the screen \(\mathscr{U}\) is generated by a family of open \(U_i\) of \(X\), giving oneself a vector fibrous \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself:

1. a vector fibre \(E_i\) on each \(U_i\),

  2. if \(U_{ij} = U_i\cap U_j = U_i\times_X U_j\), isomorphism \(\rho_{ji} : E_i|U_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} E_j|U_{ij}\), so that

  3. if \(U_{ijk} = U_i\times_X U_j\times_X U_k\), the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ E_i |U_{ijk} \ar[r]^-{\rho_{ki}|U_{ijk}} \ar[dr]_-{\rho_{ji}|U_{ijk}} & E_k|U_{ijk} \\ & E_j|U_{ijk} \ar[u]_-{\rho_{kj}|U_{ijk}} } \]

    switch, i.e. \(\rho_{ki} = \rho_{kj}\circ \rho_{ji}\) to \(U_{ijk}\).

In other words, if \(Z=\coprod U_i\) and if \(\pi:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the natural projection, giving oneself a vector fibrous \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally amounts to giving oneself:

1. a vector fibre \(E\) on \(Z\),

  2. if \(x\) and \(y\) are two points of \(Z\) such as \(\pi(x) = \pi(y)\), an isomorphism \(\rho_{y x}:E_x\xrightarrow{\sim} E_y\) between the fibres of \(E\) in \(x\) and \(y\), continuously dependent on \((x,y)\) and such,

  3. if \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\) are three points of \(Z\) such as \(\pi(x)=\pi(y)=\pi(z)\), we have \(\rho_{zx} = \rho_{zy}\circ \rho_{yx}\).

A vector fibre \(E\) on \(X\) defines a given vector fibre \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally \(E_\mathscr{U}\); the restriction system \(E_U\) from \(E\) to the objects of \(\mathscr{U}\). The fact that the vector fibre concept is local in nature can be expressed as follows: for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) from \(X\), the searcher \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{U}\), vector fibres on \(X\) in the given vector fibres \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally, is an equivalence of categories.

If in 1 we replace "open from \(X\)" with "part of \(X\)," we get the notion of the subspace sifting of \(X\). In this context also we have recollage theorems. For example: be \(X\) a space and \(\mathscr{C}\) a sifting of subspaces of \(X\) generated by a locally finished closed overlay of \(X\), then the \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{C}\), vector fibres on \(X\) in given vector fibres \(\mathscr{C}\)-locally is an equivalence of categories.
In algebraic geometry, it is useful to also consider "space cracks above \(X\)"; this is what we will see in the next paragraph.
1.4 Accurately flat descent

In the framework of the diagrams, the topology of Zariski is not fine enough for the study of nonlinear problems and it is necessary to replace in the previous definitions the immersions opened by more general morphisms. From this point of view, the techniques of descent appear as techniques of localization. Thus the following statement of descent can express by saying that the properties considered are of a local nature for the faithfully flat topology (it is said that a morphism of diagrams is faithfully flat if it is flat is overjective).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4.2

Be \(A\) a ring and \(B\) a \(A\)-algebra faithfully flat. So:

  1. A continuation \(\Sigma=(M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'')\) of \(A\)-modules is correct as soon as the continuation \(\Sigma_{(B)}\) which is deduced by extension of scalar to \(B\) is correct.

  2. A \(A\)-module \(M\) is of finite type (finite presentation, flat, locally free of finished row, invertible (i.e. locally free of rank one)) as soon as \(B\)-module \(M_{(B)}\) is.

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

(i) The \(M\mapsto M_{(B)}\) being accurate (plitude of \(B\)), it is sufficient to show that, if a \(A\)-module \(N\) is non-zero, \(N_{(B)}\) is non-zero. If \(N\) is non-zero, \(N\) contains a non-zero monogenous submodule \(A/\mathfrak{a}\); then \(N_{(B)}\) contains a monogenous submodule \((A/\mathfrak{a})_{(B)} = B/\mathfrak{a} B\), not null by overemitting structural morphism \(\varphi:\operatorname{Spec}(B)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) (if \(V(\mathfrak{a})\) is not empty, \(\varphi^{-1}(V(\mathfrak{a}))=V(\mathfrak{a} B)\) is not empty).

(ii) For any family \((x_i)\) of \(M_{(B)}\), there is a finite type submodule \(M'\) of \(M\) such that \(M'_{(B)}\) contains \(x_i\). If \(M_{(B)}\) is finite type and \(x_i\) generate \(M_{(B)}\), there is \(M'_{(B)}=M_{(B)}\), therefore \(M'=M\) and \(M\) is finite type.

C.Q.F.D.

If \(M_{(B)}\) is of finite presentation, one can, after the above, find an overjection \(A^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\). If \(N\) is the kernel of this overjection, the \(B\)-module \(N_{(B)}\) is of finite type, therefore \(N\) is of finite presentation, and \(M\) is of finite presentation. The assertion for "flat" is immediately from (i); "finite row free" means "flat and finite presentation" and the row is tested by extension of scalar to fields.

Be \(X\) a scheme and \(\mathscr{S}\) a class of \(X\)-schémas stable per fibrated product on \(X\). A class \(\mathscr{U}\subset \mathscr{S}\) is a sifting on \(X\) (relative to \(\mathscr{S}\)) if, for any morphism \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) of \(X\)-schemes, with \(U,V\in \mathscr{S}\) and \(U\in\mathscr{U}\), we have \(V\in \mathscr{U}\). The screen generated by a family \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\)-schemes in \(\mathscr{S}\) is the class of \(V\in\mathscr{S}\) such that there is a morphism of \(X\)-schemes of \(V\) in one of \(U_i\).
Either \(\mathscr{U}\) a screen on \(X\). The quasi-coherent module given \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally on \(X\) the data of

1. a quasi-coherent module \(E_U\) on each \(U\in\mathscr{U}\),

  2. for everything \(U\in\mathscr{U}\) and for any morphism \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) of \(X\)-schemes in \(\mathscr{S}\), an isomorphism \(\rho_\varphi:E_V\xrightarrow{\sim} \varphi^* E_U\), these being such that

  3. if \(\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) is a morphism of \(X\)-schemes in \(\mathscr{S}\), the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix{ E_W \ar[r]^-{\rho_{\varphi\circ\psi}} \ar[dr]_-{\rho_\psi} & \psi^*\varphi^* E_U \\ & \psi^* E_V \ar[u]_-{\psi^*\rho_\varphi} } \]

    switch, i.e. \(\rho_{\varphi\circ\psi}=(\psi^*\rho_\varphi)\circ \rho_\psi\).

If \(E\) is a quasi-coherent module on \(X\), we note \(E_\mathscr{U}\) the given module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally valid \(\varphi_U^* E\) on \(\varphi:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and such that, for any morphism \(\psi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) the restriction isomorphism \(\rho_\psi\) is canonical isomorphism \(E_V=(\varphi_U\circ \psi)^* E\xrightarrow{\sim} \psi^* \varphi_U^* E = \psi^* E_U\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.4.5

Either \(\{U_i\}\in\mathscr{S}\) a finished family of \(X\)-flats on \(X\) such that \(X\) either the reuniting of images of \(U_i\), and either \(\mathscr{U}\) the screen generated by \(\{U_i\}\). Then the \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{U}\) is an equivalence of the category of quasi-coherent modules on \(X\) with the category of quasi-coherent modules given \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally.

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

We will only deal with the case where \(x\) is affine and \(\mathscr{U}\) is generated by \(X\)-scheme affine \(U\) faithfully flat on \(X\). The reduction in this case is formal. \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\).

If the morphism \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) admits a section, \(X\) belongs to the screen \(\mathscr{U}\) and the assertion is obvious. We will reduce ourselves to this case.

A given quasi-coherent module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally defines modules \(M'\), \(M''\) and \(M'''\) on \(U\), \(U\times_X U\) and \(U\times_X U\times_X U\), and isomorphisms \(\rho:p^* M^\bullet\simeq M^\bullet\) for any projection morphism \(p\) between these spaces; this is a Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ M^* : M' \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M'' \ar[r] \ar@<4pt>[r] \ar@<-4pt>[r] & M''' } \]

above

\[ \xymatrix{ U_* : U & U\times_X U \ar@<2pt>[l] \ar@<-2pt>[l] & U\times_X U\times_X U \ar[l] \ar@<4pt>[l] \ar@<-4pt>[l] . } \]

Reciprocally \(M^*\) determines the given module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally: for \(V\in\mathscr{U}\), there is \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) and we pose \(M_U=\varphi^* M'\); for \(\varphi_1,\varphi_2:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\), we have a natural identification \(\varphi_1^* M'\simeq (\varphi_1\times \varphi_2)^* M'' \simeq \varphi_2^* M'\), and we see using \(M'''\) u these identifications are compatible, so the definition is legitimate. In short, it is up to the same to give each other a module \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally or a diagram \(M^*\) Cartesian on \(U_*\).

Translations in algebraic terms: giving oneself \(M^*\) amounts to giving oneself a Cartesian diagram of modules

\[ \xymatrix{ M' \ar@<3pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar@<-3pt>[r]|-{\partial_1} & M'' \ar@<6pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar[r]|-{\partial_1} \ar@<-6pt>[r]|-{\partial_2} & M''' } \]

above the ring diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ B \ar@<3pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar@<-3pt>[r]|-{\partial_1} & B\otimes_A B \ar@<6pt>[r]|-{\partial_0} \ar[r]|-{\partial_1} \ar@<-6pt>[r]|-{\partial_2} & B\otimes_A B\otimes_A B } \]

(species: we have \(\partial_i(b m)=\partial_i(b)\cdot\partial_i(m)\), the usual identities such as \(\partial_0\partial_1=\partial_0\partial_0\) are true, and "cardesian" means that the morphisms \(\partial_i:M'\otimes_{B,\partial_i}(B\otimes_A B)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\) and \(M''\otimes_{B\otimes_A B,\partial_i}(B\otimes_A B\otimes_A B)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'''\) are isomorphisms).

The actuator \(E\mapsto E_\mathscr{U}\) becomes the actuator that, to a \(A\)-module \(M\), associates

\[ \xymatrix{ M^* = (M\otimes_A B \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M\otimes_A B\otimes_A B \ar@<-4pt>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<4pt>[r] & M\otimes_A B\otimes_A B\otimes_A B). } \]

He admits as deputy to the right the striker

\[ \xymatrix{ (M' \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M'' \ar@<-4pt>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<4pt>[r] & M''') \ar@{|->}[r] & \ker(M' \ar@<2pt>[r] \ar@<-2pt>[r] & M'''). } \]

We have to prove that the addition arrows

\[ M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\ker(M\otimes_A B\rightrightarrows M\otimes_A B\otimes_A B) \]

and

\[ \ker(M'\rightrightarrows M'')\otimes_A B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M' \]

are isomorphisms.1.4.2.i), it is sufficient to prove it after a change of base faithfully flat \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\) (\(B\) becoming \(B'=B\otimes_A A'\)). Taking \(A'=B\), this brings us back to the case where \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) admits a section.

C.Q.F.D.
1.5 A special case: Hilbert's Theorem 90
Be \(k\) a field, \(k'\) a Galoisian extension of \(k\) and \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(k'/k)\). So homo~morphism
\begin{align*} k'\otimes_k k' &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \oplus_{\sigma\in G} k' \\ x\otimes y &\mapsto \{x\cdot \sigma(y)\}_{\sigma\in G} \end{align*}
is bijective.

It is deduced that it is the same to give oneself a module locally for the screen generated by \(\operatorname{Spec}(k')\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) or to give oneself a \(k'\)-vectorial space with a semilinear action of \(G\), i.e.:

1. a \(k'\)-vectorial space \(V'\),

  2. for all \(\sigma\in G\), an endomorphism \(\varphi_\sigma\) of the group structure of \(V'\) such as \(\varphi_\sigma(\lambda v) = \sigma(\lambda) \varphi_\sigma(v)\), for all \(\lambda\in k'\) and \(v\in V'\), checking the condition

  3. for everything \(\sigma,\tau\in G\), we have \(\varphi_{\tau\sigma} = \varphi_\tau\circ\varphi_\sigma\).
Either \(V={V'}^G\) the group of invariants by this action of \(G\); it is a \(k\)-vectorial space and, according to the theorem 1.4.5, we have:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5.2

The inclusion of \(V\) in \(V'\) defines an isomorphism \(V\otimes_k k' \xrightarrow{\sim} V'\).

In particular, if \(V'\) is of size \(1\) and \(v'\in V\) is not null, \(\varphi_\sigma\) is determined by the constant \(c(\sigma)\in {k'}^\times\) such that \(\varphi_\sigma(v')=c(\sigma)v'\) and condition (c) is written
\[ c(\tau\sigma) = c(\tau)\cdot \tau(c(\sigma))\text{.} \]
According to the proposal there is an invariant vector that is not harmful \(v=\mu v'\), \(\mu\in {k'}^\times\). Therefore we have for everything \(\sigma\in G\),
\[ c(\sigma) = \mu\cdot \sigma(\mu^{-1})\text{.} \]
In other words, everything \(1\)-cocycle of \(G\) to values in \({k'}^\times\) is a starboard:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5.3

We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(G,{k'}^\times)=0\).
1.6 Topologies of Grothendieck
We now transcribe the definitions of the preceding paragraphs into an abstract framework encompassing both the case of topological spaces and that of schematics.

Be \(\mathscr{S}\) a category and \(U\) an object of \(\mathscr{S}\). sifting on \(U\) a subset \(\mathscr{U}\) of \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{S}/U)\) such that \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belongs to \(\mathscr{U}\) and if \(\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) is a morphism in \(\mathscr{S}\), then \(\varphi\circ\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belongs to \(\mathscr{U}\).

If \(\{\varphi_i:U_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\}\) is a family of morphisms, the screen generated by \(U_i\) is by definition the set of morphisms \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) that are factored through one of \(\varphi_i\).

If \(\mathscr{U}\) is a sifter on \(U\) and if \(\varphi:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) is a morphism, the restriction \(\mathscr{U}_V\) from \(\mathscr{U}\) to \(V\) is by definition the sifter on \(V\) constituted or morphisms \(\psi:w\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) such as \(\varphi\circ\psi:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belongs to \(\mathscr{U}\).

The data of a topology of Grothendieck on \(\mathscr{S}\) consists of the data for any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\) of a set \(C(U)\) of sifting on \(U\), said sifting coverings, so that the following axioms are satisfied:

1. The screen generated by the identity of \(U\) is covered.

  2. If \(\mathscr{U}\) is a screen covering \(U\) and \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) is a morphism, the screen \(\mathscr{U}_V\) is covered.

  3. A locally covered screen is covered. In other words, if \(\mathscr{U}\) is a screen covering \(U\) and if \(\mathscr{U}'\) is a screen on \(U\) such that, for all \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belonging to \(\mathscr{U}\), the screen \(\mathscr{U}_V'\) is covered, then \(\mathscr{U}'\) is covered.
We're calling. site data from a category with Grothendieck topology.

Given a site \(\mathscr{S}\), it is called prefaisceau on \(\mathscr{S}\) a contravariant actuator \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(\mathscr{S}\) in the category of sets. For any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\), it is called section of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(U\) the elements of \(\mathscr{F}(U)\). For any morphism \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) and for all \(s\in \mathscr{F}(U)\), it is noted \(s|V\) (\(s\) restricted to \(V\)) the image of \(s\) in \(\mathscr{F}(V)\).

If \(\mathscr{U}\) is a screen on \(U\), we call the given section \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally the data, for all \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belonging to \(\mathscr{U}\), of a section \(s_V\in\mathscr{F}(V)\) such that, for any morphism \(W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\), we sit \(s_V|W=s_W\). It is said that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a sheaf if, for any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\), for any screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) on \(U\) and for any given section \(\mathscr{U}\)-locally \(\{s_V\}\), there is a single section \(s\in\mathscr{F}(U)\) such as \(s|V=s_V\), for all \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) belonging to \(\mathscr{U}\).

A similar definition is given of the abelian sheaves It is shown that the category of abelian sheaves on \(\mathscr{S}\) is an abelian category with sufficient injections. A sequence \(\mathscr{F}\xrightarrow f \mathscr{G}\xrightarrow g \mathscr{H}\) of sheaves is correct if, for any object \(U\) of \(\mathscr{S}\), and for all \(s\in \mathscr{G}(U)\) such as \(g(s)=0\), there exists locally \(t\) such as \(f(t)=s\); i.e. if there is a screen covering \(\mathscr{U}\) on \(U\) and for all \(V\in\mathscr{U}\), a section \(t_V\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(V\) such as \(f(t_V)=s|V\).
We saw two higher.

1. Be \(X\) a topological space and \(\mathscr{S}\) the category whose objects are the openings of \(X\) and morphisms natural inclusions. The topological of Grothendieck on \(\mathscr{S}\) corresponding to the usual topology of \(X\) is that for which a screen \(\mathscr{U}\) on an open \(U\) of \(X\) is covering if the meeting of the openings belonging to this screen is equal to \(U\). It is clear that the category of sheaves on \(\mathscr{S}\) is equivalent to the category of sheaves on \(X\) in the usual sense.

  2. Are \(X\) a scheme and \(\mathscr{S}\) the category of schemes on \(X\). We call topology fpqc (almost compact flat fidelity) on \(\mathscr{S}\) the topology of Grothendieck for which a screen on a \(X\)-scheme \(U\) is covering if it is generated by a finite family of flat morphisms whose images cover \(U\).
1.6.5 Cohomology

It will always be assumed that the category \(\mathscr{S}\) has a final object \(X\). Then we call the global sections of an abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\), and we note \(\Gamma\mathscr{F}\) or \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{F})\), the group \(\mathscr{F}(X)\). The \(\mathscr{F}\mapsto\Gamma\mathscr{F}\) is an exact enteror to the left of the category of abelian sheaves on \(\mathscr{S}\) in the category of abelian groups, or note \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,-)\) its derivatives (or satellites). These cohomology groups represent obstructions to move from local to global. By definition, if \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{H}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) is an exact sequence of abelian sheaves, we have an exact long sequence of cohomology:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{H}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \cdots \\ \ldots \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(X,\mathscr{H}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{n+1}(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

Given an abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(\mathscr{S}\), it is called \(\mathscr{F}\)-torsor a sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) equipped with an action \(\mathscr{F}\times\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such as locally (after restriction to all objects of a screen covering the final object \(X\)) \(\mathscr{G}\) equipped with the action of \(\mathscr{F}\) or isomorph to \(\mathscr{F}\) equipped with the canonical action \(\mathscr{F}\times \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) by translations.
It can be shown that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is interpreted as all classes with isomorphism near \(\mathscr{F}\)-torsors.

pages.title: Outlet topology
pages.html:
2 Outlet topology

The definitions of the previous chapter are specialised in the case of the spatial topology of a diagram \(X\) (2.1, 2.2, 2.3) The corresponding cohomology coincides in the case where \(X\) is the spectrum of a field \(K\) with the Galoisian cohomology of \(K\) (2.4).

2.1 Vital Topology

We will start with a few reminders about the notion of morphism.

Definition 2.1.1

Either \(A\) a ring (commutative). It is said that a \(A\)-algebra \(B\) is spread if \(B\) is a \(A\)-algebra of finite presentation and if the following equivalent conditions are verified:

  1. For all \(A\)-algebra \(C\) and for any zero square ideal \(J\) of \(C\), canonical application

    \[ \hom_{A\textnormal{-alg}}(B,C) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \hom_{A\textnormal{-alg}}(B,C/J) \]

    is a bijection.

  2. \(B\) is a \(A\)-flat module and \(\Omega_{B/A}=0\) (note \(\Omega_{B/A}\) the relative differential module).

  3. Be \(B=A[X_1,\dotsc,X_n]/I\) a presentation of \(B\). So for any first ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A[X_1,\dotsc,X_n]\) containing \(I\), there are polynomials \(P_1,\dotsc,P_n\in I\) such that \(I_\mathfrak{p}\) is generated by the images of \(P_1,\dotsc,P_n\) and \(\det(\partial P_i/\partial X_j)\notin \mathfrak{p}\).

(cf. [23] or [30])

2.4 Galoese Cohomology

For \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\) the spectrum of a field, we will see that the lethal cohomology is identified with Galoese cohomology.

2.4.1

Let's start with a topological analogy. If \(K\) is the field of functions of an affin algebraic variety integrates \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on \(\mathbb{C}\), we have \(K=\varinjlim_{f\in A} A[1/f]\).

In other words \(X=\varprojlim U\), \(U\) traversing all the openings of \(Y\). It is known that there are arbitrarily small openings of Zariski which for classical topology are \(K(\pi,1)\). Therefore, one will not be surprised if one considers \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\) itself as a \(K(\pi,1)\), \(\pi\) being the fundamental group (in the algebraic sense) of \(X\), i.e. the Galois group of \(\overline{K}/K\), where \(\overline{K}\) is separable fence of \(K\).

2.4.2

More precisely are \(K\) a field, \(\overline{K}\) a fence separable from \(K\) and \(G = \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K)\) the topological Galois group. At all \(K\)-halt finished algebra \(A\) (finished product of extensions separable from \(K\)), let's associate the finite ensemble \(\hom_K(A,\overline{K})\). The Galois group \(G\) operates on this set through a discrete quotient (so finished). If \(A=K[T]/(F)\), it identifies with all the roots in \(\overline{K}\) the polynomial \(F\). Galois theory, in the form given to it by Grothendieck, says that:

which to a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) associates its fibre \(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{K}}\) to the geometric point \(\operatorname{Spec}(\overline{K})\) is an equivalence of categories.

It is said that \(G\) operates continuously on a set \(E\) if the fixer of any element of \(E\) is an open sub-group of \(G\). The inverse-sensor is described in the obvious way: be \(A\) a \(K\)-halt finite algebra, \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(U(\overline{K})=\hom_K(A,\overline{K})\) the \(G\)-assembly corresponding to; then we have \(\mathscr{F}(U)= \hom_{G\text{-as}}(U(\overline{K}),\mathscr{F}_{\overline{K}})\).

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\text{.} \]

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Being \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) an extended morphism and \(a\in \mathbb{G}_m(U) =\Gamma(U,\mathscr{O}_U^\times)\). Since \(n\) is invertible on \(U\), the equation \(T^n-a = 0\) is separable; i.e. \(U'=\operatorname{Spec}\left(\mathscr{O}_U[T]/(T^n-a)\right)\) is spanning above \(U\). Moreover \(U'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) is surjective and has admitted a root \(n\)-second on \(U'\), hence result.

2.3 Fibres, direct images

We're calling. geometric tip of \(X\) a morphism \(\overline{x}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(\overline{x}\) is the spectrum of a separably enclosed field \(k(\overline{x})\). It will be noted abusively \(\overline{x}\), implying morphism \(\overline{x}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). If \(x\) is the image of \(\overline{x}\) in \(X\), it is said that \(\overline{x}\) is centered in \(x\). If the field \(k(\overline{x})\) is an algebraic extension of the residual field \(k(x)\), it is said that \(\overline{x}\) is a geometric point algebraic of \(X\).
We're calling. area close by of \(\overline{x}\) a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ & U \ar[d] \\ \overline{x} \ar[ur] \ar[r] & X, } \]
where \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a morphism.

The Strict localization of \(X\) en \(\overline{x}\) is the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,\overline{x}} = \varinjlim \Gamma(U,\mathscr{O}_U)\), the inductive limit being on the stalled neighborhoods of \(\overline{x}\). It is a local ring strictly Henselian whose residual field is the separable fence of the residual field \(k(x)\) de \(X\) en \(x\) in \(k(\overline{x})\). It plays the role of local ring for the spatial topology.

Given a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\), it is called fibre of \(\mathscr{F}\) en \(\overline{x}\) the whole (resp. the group,...) \(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{x}}=\varinjlim \mathscr{F}(U)\), the inductive limit being always taken on the stalled neighborhoods of \(X\).

For a sheaf homomorphism \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) to be a mono-/epi-/isomorphism it is necessary and it is sufficient that this is the morphisms \(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{x}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}_{\overline{x}}\) induced on the fibers and any geometric point of \(X\). If \(X\) is finished on an algebraically closed field, it is sufficient that it is so in the rational points of \(X\).

If \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a scheme morphism and \(\mathscr{F}\) a sheaf on \({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\), thedirect image \(f_* \mathscr{F}\) from \(\mathscr{F}\) by \(f\) is the sheaf on \({Y}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\) defined by \(f_* \mathscr{F}(V) = \mathscr{F}(X\times_Y V)\) for all \(V\) is the sheaf on \(Y\). The \(f_*:\operatorname{Sh}({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Sh}({Y}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}})\) is exact to the left. Its derived right-hand drives \(\mathsf{R}^q f_*\) are called upper direct images. If \(\overline{y}\) is a geometric point of \(Y\), we have
\[ (\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F})_{\overline{y}} = \varinjlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(V\times_Y X,\mathscr{F})\text{,} \]
Inductive limit taken on stalled neighborhoods \(V\) of \(\overline{y}\).

Been \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,\overline{y}}\) the strict localization of \(Y\) in \(\overline{y}\), \(\widetilde Y=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{Y,\overline{y}})\) and \(\widetilde X=X\times_Y \widetilde Y\). We can expand \(\mathscr{F}\) to \({\widetilde X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\) (this is a particular case of the general notion of reciprocal image) as follows: \(\widetilde U\) a schematic spread over \(\widetilde X\), so there's an outlying neighborhood \(V\) of the \(\overline{y}\) and a schematic \(U\) on \(X\times_Y V\) such as \(\widetilde U=U\times_V \widetilde Y\)We'll put it down.
\[ \mathscr{F}(\widetilde U)=\varinjlim \mathscr{F}\left(U\times_V V'\right)\text{,} \]
the inductive limit being taken on the stalled neighborhoods \(V'\) of \(\overline{y}\) which dominate \(V\). With this definition, we have
\[ \left(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\right)_{\overline{y}} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\widetilde X,\mathscr{F})\text{.} \]

The Runner \(f_*\) a deputy left \(f^*\), the "reciprocal image" spectator. \(\overline{x}\) is a geometric point of \(X\) and \(f(\overline{x})\) his image in \(Y\), we have \((f^* \mathscr{F})_{\overline{x}} = \mathscr{F}_{f(\overline{x})}\). This formula shows that \(f^*\) It's an exact striker. \(f_*\) thus transforms an injective sheaf into an injective sheaf, and the spectral sequence of the oscillator composes \(\Gamma\circ f_*\) (rep. \(g_* f_*\)) provides the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.3.4 (Leray spectral sequence)

We have \(\mathscr{F}\) an abelian sheaf on \({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\) and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of diagrams (resp. morphisms of diagrams \(X\xrightarrow f Y \xrightarrow g Z\)). We have a spectral sequence

\begin{align*} E_2^{pq} &= \underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(Y,\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}) \Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{F}) \\ \text{(rep.}\qquad E_2^{pq} &= \mathsf{R}^p g_* \mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F} \Rightarrow \mathsf{R}^{p+q}(gf)_* \mathscr{F}\text{).} \end{align*}

blocks.title: Corollary

Corollary 2.3.5

If \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}=0\) for everything \(q>0\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(Y,f_* \mathscr{F})=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(X,\mathscr{F})\) (resp. \(\mathsf{R}^p g_*(\mathscr{F}_* \mathscr{F}) = \mathsf{R}^p(g f)_* \mathscr{F}\)) for everything \(p\geqslant 0\).
This applies in particular in the following case:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.6

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a finite morphism (or even, by passing to the limit, an entire morphism) and \(\mathscr{F}\) an abelian sheaf on \(X\). Then \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}=0\), for everything \(q>0\).

Indeed be \(\overline{y}\) a geometric tip of \(Y\), \(\widetilde Y\) the spectrum of the strict locality of \(Y\) in \(y\) and \(\widetilde X=X\times_Y \widetilde Y\); from the above, it is sufficient to show that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\widetilde X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for everything \(q>0\). Or \(\widetilde X\) is the spectrum of a product of local rings strictly Henselian (cf. [30]), the \(\Gamma(\widetilde X,-)\) is correct because everything \(\widetilde X\)-scheme is both a live and a surjective one admits a section, hence the assertion.
2.4 Galoese Cohomology
For \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\) the spectrum of a field, we will see that the lethal cohomology is identified with Galoese cohomology.

Let's start with a topological analogy. If \(K\) is the field of functions of an affin algebraic variety integrates \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on \(\mathbb{C}\), we have \(K=\varinjlim_{f\in A} A[1/f]\).

In other words \(X=\varprojlim U\), \(U\) traversing all the openings of \(Y\). It is known that there are arbitrarily small openings of Zariski which for classical topology are \(K(\pi,1)\). Therefore, one will not be surprised if one considers \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\) itself as a \(K(\pi,1)\), \(\pi\) being the fundamental group (in the algebraic sense) of \(X\), i.e. the Galois group of \(\overline{K}/K\), where \(\overline{K}\) is separable fence of \(K\).

More precisely are \(K\) a field, \(\overline{K}\) a fence separable from \(K\) and \(G = \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K)\) the topological Galois group. At all \(K\)-halt finished algebra \(A\) (finished product of extensions separable from \(K\)), let's associate the finite ensemble \(\hom_K(A,\overline{K})\). The Galois group \(G\) operates on this set through a discrete quotient (so finished). If \(A=K[T]/(F)\), it identifies with all the roots in \(\overline{K}\) the polynomial \(F\). Galois theory, in the form given to it by Grothendieck, says that:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4.3

The Runner

\[ \left\{\begin{array}{c} K \textnormal{-Algebras} \\ \textnormal{finished stalls} \end{array}\right\} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \left\{\begin{array}{c} \textnormal{finished sets on which} \\ G \textnormal{ continuously operates} \end{array}\right\} \]

which to an algebra \(A\) associates \(\hom_K(A,\overline{K})\) is an anti-equivalence of categories.
A similar description of the sheaves for the spreading topology is derived on \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4.4

The Runner

\[ \left\{\begin{array}{c} \textnormal{Stall sheaves} \\ \textnormal{on } \operatorname{Spec}(K) \end{array}\right\} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \left\{\begin{array}{c} \textnormal{groups on which} \\ G \textnormal{ continuously operates} \end{array}\right\} \]

which to a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) associates its fibre \(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{K}}\) to the geometric point \(\operatorname{Spec}(\overline{K})\) is an equivalence of categories.

It is said that \(G\) operates continuously on a set \(E\) if the fixer of any element of \(E\) is an open sub-group of \(G\). The inverse-sensor is described in the obvious way: be \(A\) a \(K\)-halt finite algebra, \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(U(\overline{K})=\hom_K(A,\overline{K})\) the \(G\)-assembly corresponding to; then we have \(\mathscr{F}(U)= \hom_{G\text{-as}}(U(\overline{K}),\mathscr{F}_{\overline{K}})\).

In particular, if \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(X) = \mathscr{F}_{\overline{K}}^G\). If we restrict ourselves to the abelian sheaves, we obtain by passing to the searchers derived from canonical isomorphisms
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}},\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(G,\mathscr{F}_{\overline{K}}) \]

1. The constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) corresponds \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) with trivial action of \(G\).

  2. To the root sheaf \(n\)ths of the unit \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) corresponds the group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n(\overline{K})\) of the roots \(n\)ths of the unit in \(\overline{K}\), with the natural action of \(G\).

  3. To the sheaf \(\mathbb{G}_m\) corresponds the group \(\overline{K}^\times\) with the natural action of \(G\).

pages.title: Cohomology of curves
pages.html:
3 Cohomology of curves

In the case of topological spaces, stripping using the Künneth formula and simplical decompositions allow to return to calculate the cohomology at the interval \(I=[0,1]\) for which we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(I,\mathbb{Z})=\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(I,\mathbb{Z})=0\) for \(q>0\).

In our case, the strippings will result in more complicated objects, i.e. curves on an algebraically closed field; we will calculate their cohomology in this chapter. The situation is more complex than in the topological case because the cohomology groups are harmful to \(q>2\) only. The essential ingredient of the calculations is the invalidity of the Brauer group of the functions of such a curve (theorem of Tsen, 3.2).

3.1 The Brauer Group

First of all, let us recall the classic definition:

Definition 3.1.1

Either \(K\) a field and \(A\) a \(K\)-algebra of finite dimension. It is said that \(A\) is a simple central algebra on \(K\) if the following equivalent conditions are verified:

  1. \(A\) has no nontrivial bilateral ideal and its centre is \(K\).

  2. There is a finished Galoisian extension \(K'/K\) such that \(A_{K'} = A\otimes_K K'\) is isomorphic to an algebra of square matrices on \(K'\).

  3. \(A\) is \(K\)-isomorph to algebra of square matrices on a center left field \(K\).

Two such algebras are said to be equivalent if the left fields associated with them by (c) are \(K\)-isomorphs. If these algebras out even size, this amounts to saying that they are \(K\)-isomorphs. The tensor product defines by passing to the quotient an abelian group structure on all equivalence classes. It is this group that is called classically the Brauer Group of \(K\) and noted \(\operatorname{Br}(K)\).

3.1.2

Note: \(\operatorname{Br}(n,K)\) the total classes of \(K\)-isomorphism of \(K\)-Algebras \(A\) such that there is a finished Galoisian extension \(K'\) of the \(K\) for which \(A_{K'}\) is isomorphic to algebra \(M_n(K')\) square matrices \(n\times n\) on \(K'\). By definition \(\operatorname{Br}(K)\) is meeting of the sub-assemblies \(\operatorname{Br}(n,K)\) for \(n\in\mathbb{N}\). Be \(\overline{K}\) an algebraic fence of \(K\) and \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K)\). The whole \(\operatorname{Br}(n,K)\) is all the "forms" of \(M_n(\overline{K})\), it is canonically isomorphic to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{Aut}(M_n(\overline{K}))\right)\).

We know that any automorphism of \(M_n(\overline{K})\) is internal. Therefore the group \(\operatorname{Aut}(M_n(\overline{K}))\) is identified with the projective linear group \(\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\) and we have a canonical bijection:

\[ \theta_n : \operatorname{Br}(n,K) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right)\text{.} \]

On the other hand, the exact follow-up:

\[ \tag{3.1.2.1} 1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{K}^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(n,\overline{K}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1\text{,} \]

allows to define a port operator:

\[ \Delta_n : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\text{.} \]

By dialling \(\theta_n\) and \(\Delta_n\), you get an application:

\[ \delta_n : \operatorname{Br}(n,K) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\text{.} \]

It is easy to check that \(\delta_n\) applications are compatible with each other and define group homomorphism:

\[ \delta : \operatorname{Br}(K) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\text{.} \]
Proposition 3.1.3

Homomorphism \(\delta:\operatorname{Br}(K)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\) is bijective.

This results from the following two lines:

Lemma 3.1.4

The application \(\Delta_n:\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\) is injective.

Based on [36], cor. à la prop. I-44, it is sufficient to verify that every time we twist the exact sequence (3.1.2.1) by an element of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right)\), the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) of the median group is trivial. This median group is the group of \(\overline{K}\)-points of the multiplicative group of a single central algebra \(A\) of rank \(n^2\) of \(K\). To prove that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(G,A_{\overline{K}}^\times) = 0\), n interpret \(A^\times\) as the group of automorphisms of \(A\)-free module \(L\) of rank \(1\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) as all the "forms" of \(L\) – of \(A\)-modules of rank \(n^2\) of \(K\), automatically free.

Lemma 3.1.5

Be \(\alpha\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\), \(K'\) a finite extension of \(K\) contained in \(\overline{K}\), \(n=[K':K]\), and \(G'=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K')\). If the image of \(\alpha\) in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G',\overline{K}^\times)\) is null, then \(\alpha\) belongs to the image of \(\Delta_n\).

First of all, let's note that we have:

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G',\overline{K}^\times) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2\left(G,(\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times\right)\text{.} \]

(From a geometric point of view if we note \(x=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\), \(x'=\operatorname{Spec}(K')\) and \(\pi:x'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} x\) canonical morphism, we have \(\mathsf{R}^q \pi_*(\mathbb{G}_{m,x'}) = 0\) for \(q>0\) and then \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(x',\mathbb{G}_{m,X'})\simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(x,\pi_*\mathbb{G}_{m,X'})\) for \(q\geqslant 0\)).

Moreover, the choice of a database of \(K'\) as a vector space on \(K\) allows you to define homomorphism

\[ (\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(n,\overline{K}) \]

which, to an element \(x\), matches the multiplication endomorphism by \(x\) of \(\overline{K}\otimes_K K'\). We then have a commutative diagram with exact lines:

\[ \xymatrix{ 1 \ar[r] & \overline{K}^\times \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & (\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times \ar[r] \ar[d] & (\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times / \overline{K}^\times \ar[r] \ar[d] & 1 \\ 1 \ar[r] & \overline{K}^\times \ar[r] & \operatorname{gl}(n,\overline{K}) \ar[r] & \operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K}) \ar[r] & 1 } \]

The lemma is the result of the commutative diagram which is deduced by moving to the cohomology:

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,(\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times/\overline{K}^\times\right) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2\left(G,(\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times\right) \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right) \ar[r]^-{\Delta_n} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times) . } \]
Proposition 3.1.6

Be \(K\) a field, \(\overline{K}\) an algebraic fence of \(K\) and \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K)\). Suppose that, for any finite extension \(K'\) of \(K\), we have \(\operatorname{Br}(K')=0\). Then we have:

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(G,\overline{K}^\times) = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).

  2. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(G,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(G\)-torsion module \(\mathscr{F}\) and for all \(q\geqslant 2\).

(For the demonstration, see [36]).

3.2 Tsen's Theorem

Definition 3.2.1

It is said that a field \(K\) is \(C_1\) if any homogeneous polynomial not constant \(f(x_1,\dotsc,x_n)\) degree \(d<n\) has a nontrivial zero.

Proposition 3.2.2

If a field \(K\) is \(C_1\), we have \(\operatorname{Br}(K)=0\).

It is to show that any left field \(D\) centre \(K\) and finished on \(K\) is equal to \(K\). Be \(r^2\) the degree of \(D\) on \(K\) and \(\operatorname{Nrd}:D\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) the reduced standard.

(Locally for the spatial topology on \(K\), \(D\) is isomorphs – not canonically – to an algebra of matrices \(M_r\) and the reduced standard coincides with the determinant application. This application is well defined, regardless of the isomorphism chosen between \(D\) and \(M_r\) because any automorphism of \(M_r\) is internal and two similar matrices even have a determinant. This locally defined application for the spatial topology descends, because of its local uniqueness, in an application \(\operatorname{Nrd}:D\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\)).

The only zero of \(\operatorname{Nrd}\) is the null element of \(D\), because, if \(x\ne 0\), we have \(\operatorname{Nrd}(x)\cdot \operatorname{Nrd}(x^{-1}) = 1\). On the other hand, if \(\{e_1,\dotsc,e_{r^2}\}\) is a basis of \(D\) on \(K\) and if \(x=\sum x_i e_i\), the function \(\operatorname{Nrd}(x)\) is written as a homogeneous polynomial \(\operatorname{Nrd}(x_1,\dotsc,x_{r^2})\) degree \(r\) (it is clear locally for the lethal topology). Since \(K\) is \(C_1\), we have \(r^2\leqslant r\), i.e. \(r=1\) and \(D=K\).

Theorem 3.2.3 (Tsen)

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field and \(K\) an extension of degree of transcendence \(1\) of \(k\). So \(K\) is \(C_1\).

Let's first assume that \(K=k(X)\).

\[ f(T) = \sum a_{i_1,\dotsc i_n} T_1^{i_1} \dotsm T_n^{i_n} \]

a homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d<n\) with coefficients in \(k(X)\). Quit multiplying the coefficients by a common denominator it can be assumed that they are in \(k[X]\). Then either \(\delta=\sup\deg(a_{i_1\dotsc i_n})\). We're looking for a non-trivial zero in \(k[X]\) by the method of indeterminate coefficients by writing each \(T_i\) (\(i=1,\dotsc,n\)) as a polynomial of degree \(N\) in \(X\). Then the equation \(f(T)=0\) becomes a system of homogenous equations in \(n\times (N+1)\) coefficients of polynomials \(T_i(X)\) expressing the nullity of the coefficients of the polynomial in \(X\) obtained by replacing \(T_i\) by \(T_i(X)\). This polynomial is of degree \(\delta+N D\) at most, so there's \(\delta+N d+1\) equations in \(n\times (N+1)\) variables. \(k\) is algebraically closed this system has a nontrivial solution if \(n(N+1)>N d+\delta+1\), which will be the case for \(N\) big enough if \(d<n\).

It is clear that, to demonstrate the theorem in the general case, it is sufficient to demonstrate this when \(K\) is a finite extension of a pure transcendent extension \(k(X)\) of the \(k\). Either \(f(T)=f(T_1,\dotsc,T_n)\) a homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d<n\) with coefficients in \(K\). Be \(a=[K:k(X)]\) and \(e_1,\dotsc,e_s\) a bottom of \(K\) on \(k(X)\). Introduction of new variables \(U_{i j}\), by number \(s n\), such as \(T_i=\sum U_{i j}e_j\). To make the polynomial \(f(T)\) have a non-trivial zero in \(K\), it is enough that the polynomial \(g(X_{i j}) = N_{K/k}(f( T))\) have a non-trivial zero in \(k(X)\)Or. \(g\) is a polynomial of degree \(s d\) in \(s n\) variable, hence the result.

Corollary 3.2.4

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field and \(K\) an extension of degree of transcendence \(1\) of \(k\). So the groups of lethal cohomology \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\operatorname{Spec}(K),\mathbb{G}_m)\) are null for everything \(q>0\).

Cohomology of smooth curves

From now on, and unless expressly stated otherwise, the cohomology groups considered are the groups of the extended cohomology.

Proposition 3.3.1

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field and \(X\) a related projective curve not singular on \(k\). So we have:

\begin{align*} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathbb{G}_m) &= k^\times \text{,} \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{G}_m) &= \operatorname{Pic}(X) \text{,} \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{G}_m) &= 0 \text{ for } q\geqslant 2 \text{.} \end{align*}

We have \(\eta\) the generic point of \(X\), \(j:\eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism and \(\mathbb{G}_{m,X}\) the multiplicative group of the fractional field \(K(X)\). For any closed point \(x\) of \(X\), be \(i_x:x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion and \(\mathbb{Z}_x\) the constant sheaf of value \(\mathbb{Z}\) on \(x\). Thus \(j_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}\) is the sheaf of non-zero meromorphic functions on \(X\) and \(\oplus_{x\in X} i_{x_I}\mathbb{Z}_x\) the sheaf of divisors, so we have an exact sequence of sheaves:

\[ \tag{3.3.1.1} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & j_* \mathbb{G}_{m,\eta} \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{div}} \ar[r] & \displaystyle\bigoplus_{x\in X} i_{x*} \mathbb{Z}_x \ar[r] & 0. } \]
Lemma 3.3.2

We have \(\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{G}_{m,\eta} = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).

It is enough to show that the fiber of this sheaf at all ends closed \(x\) of \(X\) is zero. If \(\widetilde{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x}\) is the hex of \(X\) in \(x\) and \(K\) the field of the fractions of \(\widetilde{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x}\), we have

\[ \operatorname{Spec}(K) = \eta\times_X \operatorname{Spec}(\widetilde{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x})\text{,} \]

So \((\mathsf{R}^qj_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta})_x = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\operatorname{Spec}(K),\mathbb{G}_m)\).

Now \(K\) is an algebraic extension of \(k(X)\), therefore an extension of the degree of transcendent \(1\) of \(k\): the lemma results from (3.2.4).

Lemma 3.3.3

We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X, j_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).

In fact, it is not the case.3.3.2) and of the Leray spectral sequence for \(j\), we deduce:

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X, j_* \mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\eta,\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) \]

for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and the second member is void for \(q>0\) after (3.2.4).

Lemma 3.3.4

We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q\left(X,\bigoplus_{x\in X} i_{x*} \mathbb{Z}_x\right) = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).

Indeed for any closed point of \(X\), we have \(\mathsf{R}^q i_{x*}\mathbb{Z}_x 0\) for \(q>0\), because \(i_x\) is a finite morphism (2.3.6), and

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,i_{x*}\mathbb{Z}_x) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(x,\mathbb{Z}_x)\text{.} \]

The second member is void for everything \(q>0\), because \(x\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field (We see that the lemma is more generally true for any "scratch" sheaf on \(X\)).

Previous lemmes and the exact sequence are deduced (3.3.1.1) equalities:

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{G}_m) = 0 \text{ for } q\geqslant 2 \text{,} \]

and an exact suite of low-degree cohomology:

\[ 1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathbb{G}_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X, j_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\left(X,\bigoplus_{x\in X} i_{x*}\mathbb{Z}_x\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{G}_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1 \]

which is none other than the exact follow-up:
