\[ 1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k(X)^\times\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Div}(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1\text{.} \]

The proposal 3.3.1 It is deduced that the cohomology groups of \(X\) at value in \(\mathbb{Z}/n\), \(n\) first to the characteristic of \(k\), have a reasonable value:

Corollary 3.3.5

If \(X\) is of genus \(g\) and \(n\) is invertible in \(k\), \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is void for \(q>2\), and free for \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) of rank \(1,2 g,1\) for \(q=0,1,2\). Replace \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) by the isomorph group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), there are canonical isomorphs

\begin{align*} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) &= \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) &= \operatorname{Pic}^0(X)_n \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) &= \mathbb{Z}/n \text{.} \end{align*}

As the field \(k\) is algebraically closed, \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) is is isomorphic (not canonically) to \(\mu_n\). From the exact continuation of Kummer:

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mu_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\text{,} \]

and the proposal 3.3.1, equality is deduced:

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0 \text{ for } q>2 \text{,} \]

and, in low degree, exact follow-up:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r] & k^\times \ar[r]^-n & k^\times \ar[r] & 0 } \]
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}(X) \ar[r]^-n & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r] & 0. } \]

In addition, we have an exact follow-up:

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}^0(X) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}(X) \ar[r]^-{\deg} & \mathbb{Z} \ar[r] & 0 , } \]

and \(\operatorname{Pic}^0(X)\) is identified with the group of rational points on \(k\) of an abelian variety of size \(g\), the jacobian of \(X\). In such a group, the multiplication by \(n\) is overjective and its kernel is a \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-free module of rank \(2 g\) (because \(n\) is invertible in \(k\)); hence the corollary.

From clever unscrewing, using the "track method," it is possible to obtain in corollary the

Proposition 3.3.6 ([2])

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field, \(X\) an algebraic curve on \(k\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). So:

  1. We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(q>2\).

  2. If \(X\) is affine, we even have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(q>1\).

For the demonstration, as well as for the presentation of the "track method," we refer to [2].

3.4 Screws

To calculate the cohomology of size varieties \(>1\) fibrations are used by curves, which allows us to reduce ourselves to studying morphisms whose fibers are of dimension \(\leqslant 1\). This principle has several variants, let's say a few.

3.4.1

Be \(A\) a \(k\)-algebra of finite type and \(a_1,\dotsc,a_n\) generators of \(A\). If you place \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(X_i=\operatorname{Spec}(k[a_1,\dotsc,a_i])\), \(X_n=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the canonical inclusions \(k[a_1,\dotsc,a_i]\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k[a_1,\dotsc,a_i,a_{i+1}]\) define morphisms \(X_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n-1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\) whose fibres are of size \(\leqslant 1\).

3.4.2

In the case of smooth morphism, we can be more precise. elemental fibration a schematic morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) that can be immersed in a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j \ar[dr]_-f & \overline{X} \ar[d]^-{\overline{f}} & Y \ar@{_{(}->}[l]_-i \ar[dl]^-g\\ & S } \]

meeting the following conditions:

  1. \(j\) is a dense open immersion in each fiber and \(X=\overline{X} \setminus Y\).

  2. \(\overline{f}\) is smooth and projective, with irreducible geometrical fibres and dimensions \(1\).

  3. \(g\) is a surface and no fibres of \(g\) are empty.

We're calling. Good neighborhood concerning \(S\) a \(S\)-scheme \(X\) as it exists \(S\)-schemes \(X=X_n,\dotsc,X_0=S\) and elementary fibrations \(f_i:X_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{i-1}\), \(i=1,\dotsc,n\). [2] that if \(X\) is a smooth pattern on an algebraically closed field \(k\) any rational point of \(X\) has an open neighborhood which is a good neighborhood (relating to \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\)).

3.4.3

One can unscrew a proper morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in the following way. According to Chow's lemma, there is a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[dr]_-f & \overline{X} \ar[l]_-\pi \ar[d]^-{\overline{f}}\\ & S } \]

where \(\pi\) and \(\overline{f}\) are projective morphisms, \(\pi\) being in addition an isomorphism above a dense open \(X\). Locally on \(S\), \(\overline{X}\) is a closed sub-scheme of a projective space type \(\mathbb{P}_S^n\).

The latter is unscrewed by considering the projection \(\varphi:\mathbb{P}_S^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbb{P}_S^1\) which sends the point of homogenous coordinates \((x_0,x_1,\dotsc,x_n)\) on \((x_0,x_1)\). It is a rational application defined outside the closed \(Y\simeq \mathbb{P}_S^{n-2}\) of \(\mathbb{P}_S^n\) of homogeneous equations \(x_0=x_1=0\). Either \(u:P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{P}_S^n\) the center burst \(Y\); the fibres of \(u\) either of size \(\leqslant 1\). Moreover, there is a natural morphism \(v:P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{P}_S^1\) which prolongs the rational application \(\varphi\) and \(v\) makes \(P\) a \(\mathbb{P}_S^1\)-scheme locally isomorphic to the projective space type \(\mathbb{P}^{n-1}\) which can be projected on \(\mathbb{P}^1\), etc.

3.4.4

One can sweep a projective and smooth variety \(X\) by a Lefschetz brush. The burst \(\widetilde X\) of the intersection of the brush axis with \(X\) is projected on \(\mathbb{P}^1\) and the fibers of this projection are the hyperplane sections of \(X\) by the hyperplanes of the brush.

blocks.html: 3 Cohomology of curves

In the case of topological spaces, stripping using the Künneth formula and simplical decompositions allow to return to calculate the cohomology at the interval \(I=[0,1]\) for which we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(I,\mathbb{Z})=\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(I,\mathbb{Z})=0\) for \(q>0\).

In our case, the strippings will result in more complicated objects, i.e. curves on an algebraically closed field; we will calculate their cohomology in this chapter. The situation is more complex than in the topological case because the cohomology groups are harmful to \(q>2\) only. The essential ingredient of the calculations is the invalidity of the Brauer group of the functions of such a curve (theorem of Tsen, 3.2).
3.1 The Brauer Group
First of all, let us recall the classic definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.1

Either \(K\) a field and \(A\) a \(K\)-algebra of finite dimension. It is said that \(A\) is a simple central algebra on \(K\) if the following equivalent conditions are verified:

  1. \(A\) has no nontrivial bilateral ideal and its centre is \(K\).

  2. There is a finished Galoisian extension \(K'/K\) such that \(A_{K'} = A\otimes_K K'\) is isomorphic to an algebra of square matrices on \(K'\).

  3. \(A\) is \(K\)-isomorph to algebra of square matrices on a center left field \(K\).

Two such algebras are said to be equivalent if the left fields associated with them by (c) are \(K\)-isomorphs. If these algebras out even size, this amounts to saying that they are \(K\)-isomorphs. The tensor product defines by passing to the quotient an abelian group structure on all equivalence classes. It is this group that is called classically the Brauer Group of \(K\) and noted \(\operatorname{Br}(K)\).

Note: \(\operatorname{Br}(n,K)\) the total classes of \(K\)-isomorphism of \(K\)-Algebras \(A\) such that there is a finished Galoisian extension \(K'\) of the \(K\) for which \(A_{K'}\) is isomorphic to algebra \(M_n(K')\) square matrices \(n\times n\) on \(K'\). By definition \(\operatorname{Br}(K)\) is meeting of the sub-assemblies \(\operatorname{Br}(n,K)\) for \(n\in\mathbb{N}\). Be \(\overline{K}\) an algebraic fence of \(K\) and \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K)\). The whole \(\operatorname{Br}(n,K)\) is all the "forms" of \(M_n(\overline{K})\), it is canonically isomorphic to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{Aut}(M_n(\overline{K}))\right)\).

We know that any automorphism of \(M_n(\overline{K})\) is internal. Therefore the group \(\operatorname{Aut}(M_n(\overline{K}))\) is identified with the projective linear group \(\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\) and we have a canonical bijection:
\[ \theta_n : \operatorname{Br}(n,K) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right)\text{.} \]
On the other hand, the exact follow-up:

\[ \tag{3.1.2.1} 1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{K}^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(n,\overline{K}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1\text{,} \]
allows to define a port operator:

\[ \Delta_n : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\text{.} \]
By dialling \(\theta_n\) and \(\Delta_n\), you get an application:
\[ \delta_n : \operatorname{Br}(n,K) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\text{.} \]
It is easy to check that \(\delta_n\) applications are compatible with each other and define group homomorphism:
\[ \delta : \operatorname{Br}(K) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\text{.} \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1.3

Homomorphism \(\delta:\operatorname{Br}(K)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\) is bijective.
This results from the following two lines:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.4

The application \(\Delta_n:\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\) is injective.

Based on [36], cor. à la prop. I-44, it is sufficient to verify that every time we twist the exact sequence (3.1.2.1) by an element of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right)\), the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) of the median group is trivial. This median group is the group of \(\overline{K}\)-points of the multiplicative group of a single central algebra \(A\) of rank \(n^2\) of \(K\). To prove that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(G,A_{\overline{K}}^\times) = 0\), n interpret \(A^\times\) as the group of automorphisms of \(A\)-free module \(L\) of rank \(1\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\) as all the "forms" of \(L\) – of \(A\)-modules of rank \(n^2\) of \(K\), automatically free.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.5

Be \(\alpha\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times)\), \(K'\) a finite extension of \(K\) contained in \(\overline{K}\), \(n=[K':K]\), and \(G'=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K')\). If the image of \(\alpha\) in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G',\overline{K}^\times)\) is null, then \(\alpha\) belongs to the image of \(\Delta_n\).
First of all, let's note that we have:
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G',\overline{K}^\times) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2\left(G,(\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times\right)\text{.} \]

(From a geometric point of view if we note \(x=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\), \(x'=\operatorname{Spec}(K')\) and \(\pi:x'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} x\) canonical morphism, we have \(\mathsf{R}^q \pi_*(\mathbb{G}_{m,x'}) = 0\) for \(q>0\) and then \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(x',\mathbb{G}_{m,X'})\simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(x,\pi_*\mathbb{G}_{m,X'})\) for \(q\geqslant 0\)).
Moreover, the choice of a database of \(K'\) as a vector space on \(K\) allows you to define homomorphism
\[ (\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gl}(n,\overline{K}) \]
which, to an element \(x\), matches the multiplication endomorphism by \(x\) of \(\overline{K}\otimes_K K'\). We then have a commutative diagram with exact lines:

\[ \xymatrix{ 1 \ar[r] & \overline{K}^\times \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & (\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times \ar[r] \ar[d] & (\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times / \overline{K}^\times \ar[r] \ar[d] & 1 \\ 1 \ar[r] & \overline{K}^\times \ar[r] & \operatorname{gl}(n,\overline{K}) \ar[r] & \operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K}) \ar[r] & 1 } \]
The lemma is the result of the commutative diagram which is deduced by moving to the cohomology:

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,(\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times/\overline{K}^\times\right) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2\left(G,(\overline{K}\otimes_K K')^\times\right) \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(G,\operatorname{PGL}(n,\overline{K})\right) \ar[r]^-{\Delta_n} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(G,\overline{K}^\times) . } \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1.6

Be \(K\) a field, \(\overline{K}\) an algebraic fence of \(K\) and \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{K}/K)\). Suppose that, for any finite extension \(K'\) of \(K\), we have \(\operatorname{Br}(K')=0\). Then we have:

  1. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(G,\overline{K}^\times) = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).

  2. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(G,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(G\)-torsion module \(\mathscr{F}\) and for all \(q\geqslant 2\).
(For the demonstration, see [36]).
3.2 Tsen's Theorem

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2.1

It is said that a field \(K\) is \(C_1\) if any homogeneous polynomial not constant \(f(x_1,\dotsc,x_n)\) degree \(d<n\) has a nontrivial zero.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2.2

If a field \(K\) is \(C_1\), we have \(\operatorname{Br}(K)=0\).

It is to show that any left field \(D\) centre \(K\) and finished on \(K\) is equal to \(K\). Be \(r^2\) the degree of \(D\) on \(K\) and \(\operatorname{Nrd}:D\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) the reduced standard.

(Locally for the spatial topology on \(K\), \(D\) is isomorphs – not canonically – to an algebra of matrices \(M_r\) and the reduced standard coincides with the determinant application. This application is well defined, regardless of the isomorphism chosen between \(D\) and \(M_r\) because any automorphism of \(M_r\) is internal and two similar matrices even have a determinant. This locally defined application for the spatial topology descends, because of its local uniqueness, in an application \(\operatorname{Nrd}:D\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\)).

The only zero of \(\operatorname{Nrd}\) is the null element of \(D\), because, if \(x\ne 0\), we have \(\operatorname{Nrd}(x)\cdot \operatorname{Nrd}(x^{-1}) = 1\). On the other hand, if \(\{e_1,\dotsc,e_{r^2}\}\) is a basis of \(D\) on \(K\) and if \(x=\sum x_i e_i\), the function \(\operatorname{Nrd}(x)\) is written as a homogeneous polynomial \(\operatorname{Nrd}(x_1,\dotsc,x_{r^2})\) degree \(r\) (it is clear locally for the lethal topology). Since \(K\) is \(C_1\), we have \(r^2\leqslant r\), i.e. \(r=1\) and \(D=K\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2.3 (Tsen)

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field and \(K\) an extension of degree of transcendence \(1\) of \(k\). So \(K\) is \(C_1\).
Let's first assume that \(K=k(X)\).
\[ f(T) = \sum a_{i_1,\dotsc i_n} T_1^{i_1} \dotsm T_n^{i_n} \]

a homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d<n\) with coefficients in \(k(X)\). Quit multiplying the coefficients by a common denominator it can be assumed that they are in \(k[X]\). Then either \(\delta=\sup\deg(a_{i_1\dotsc i_n})\). We're looking for a non-trivial zero in \(k[X]\) by the method of indeterminate coefficients by writing each \(T_i\) (\(i=1,\dotsc,n\)) as a polynomial of degree \(N\) in \(X\). Then the equation \(f(T)=0\) becomes a system of homogenous equations in \(n\times (N+1)\) coefficients of polynomials \(T_i(X)\) expressing the nullity of the coefficients of the polynomial in \(X\) obtained by replacing \(T_i\) by \(T_i(X)\). This polynomial is of degree \(\delta+N D\) at most, so there's \(\delta+N d+1\) equations in \(n\times (N+1)\) variables. \(k\) is algebraically closed this system has a nontrivial solution if \(n(N+1)>N d+\delta+1\), which will be the case for \(N\) big enough if \(d<n\).

It is clear that, to demonstrate the theorem in the general case, it is sufficient to demonstrate this when \(K\) is a finite extension of a pure transcendent extension \(k(X)\) of the \(k\). Either \(f(T)=f(T_1,\dotsc,T_n)\) a homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d<n\) with coefficients in \(K\). Be \(a=[K:k(X)]\) and \(e_1,\dotsc,e_s\) a bottom of \(K\) on \(k(X)\). Introduction of new variables \(U_{i j}\), by number \(s n\), such as \(T_i=\sum U_{i j}e_j\). To make the polynomial \(f(T)\) have a non-trivial zero in \(K\), it is enough that the polynomial \(g(X_{i j}) = N_{K/k}(f( T))\) have a non-trivial zero in \(k(X)\)Or. \(g\) is a polynomial of degree \(s d\) in \(s n\) variable, hence the result.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2.4

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field and \(K\) an extension of degree of transcendence \(1\) of \(k\). So the groups of lethal cohomology \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\operatorname{Spec}(K),\mathbb{G}_m)\) are null for everything \(q>0\).
Cohomology of smooth curves
From now on, and unless expressly stated otherwise, the cohomology groups considered are the groups of the extended cohomology.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3.1

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field and \(X\) a related projective curve not singular on \(k\). So we have:

\begin{align*} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathbb{G}_m) &= k^\times \text{,} \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{G}_m) &= \operatorname{Pic}(X) \text{,} \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{G}_m) &= 0 \text{ for } q\geqslant 2 \text{.} \end{align*}

We have \(\eta\) the generic point of \(X\), \(j:\eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism and \(\mathbb{G}_{m,X}\) the multiplicative group of the fractional field \(K(X)\). For any closed point \(x\) of \(X\), be \(i_x:x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion and \(\mathbb{Z}_x\) the constant sheaf of value \(\mathbb{Z}\) on \(x\). Thus \(j_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}\) is the sheaf of non-zero meromorphic functions on \(X\) and \(\oplus_{x\in X} i_{x_I}\mathbb{Z}_x\) the sheaf of divisors, so we have an exact sequence of sheaves:
\[ \tag{3.3.1.1} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & j_* \mathbb{G}_{m,\eta} \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{div}} \ar[r] & \displaystyle\bigoplus_{x\in X} i_{x*} \mathbb{Z}_x \ar[r] & 0. } \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3.2

We have \(\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{G}_{m,\eta} = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).

It is enough to show that the fiber of this sheaf at all ends closed \(x\) of \(X\) is zero. If \(\widetilde{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x}\) is the hex of \(X\) in \(x\) and \(K\) the field of the fractions of \(\widetilde{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x}\), we have
\[ \operatorname{Spec}(K) = \eta\times_X \operatorname{Spec}(\widetilde{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x})\text{,} \]
So \((\mathsf{R}^qj_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta})_x = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\operatorname{Spec}(K),\mathbb{G}_m)\).
Now \(K\) is an algebraic extension of \(k(X)\), therefore an extension of the degree of transcendent \(1\) of \(k\): the lemma results from (3.2.4).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3.3

We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X, j_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).
In fact, it is not the case.3.3.2) and of the Leray spectral sequence for \(j\), we deduce:
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X, j_* \mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\eta,\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) \]
for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and the second member is void for \(q>0\) after (3.2.4).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3.4

We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q\left(X,\bigoplus_{x\in X} i_{x*} \mathbb{Z}_x\right) = 0\) for everything \(q>0\).
Indeed for any closed point of \(X\), we have \(\mathsf{R}^q i_{x*}\mathbb{Z}_x 0\) for \(q>0\), because \(i_x\) is a finite morphism (2.3.6), and
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,i_{x*}\mathbb{Z}_x) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(x,\mathbb{Z}_x)\text{.} \]
The second member is void for everything \(q>0\), because \(x\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field (We see that the lemma is more generally true for any "scratch" sheaf on \(X\)).
Previous lemmes and the exact sequence are deduced (3.3.1.1) equalities:
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{G}_m) = 0 \text{ for } q\geqslant 2 \text{,} \]
and an exact suite of low-degree cohomology:

\[ 1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathbb{G}_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X, j_*\mathbb{G}_{m,\eta}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\left(X,\bigoplus_{x\in X} i_{x*}\mathbb{Z}_x\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{G}_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1 \]
which is none other than the exact follow-up:

\[ 1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^\times \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k(X)^\times\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Div}(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1\text{.} \]
The proposal 3.3.1 It is deduced that the cohomology groups of \(X\) at value in \(\mathbb{Z}/n\), \(n\) first to the characteristic of \(k\), have a reasonable value:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3.5

If \(X\) is of genus \(g\) and \(n\) is invertible in \(k\), \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is void for \(q>2\), and free for \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) of rank \(1,2 g,1\) for \(q=0,1,2\). Replace \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) by the isomorph group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), there are canonical isomorphs

\begin{align*} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) &= \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) &= \operatorname{Pic}^0(X)_n \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) &= \mathbb{Z}/n \text{.} \end{align*}
As the field \(k\) is algebraically closed, \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) is is isomorphic (not canonically) to \(\mu_n\). From the exact continuation of Kummer:

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mu_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\text{,} \]
and the proposal 3.3.1, equality is deduced:
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0 \text{ for } q>2 \text{,} \]
and, in low degree, exact follow-up:
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r] & k^\times \ar[r]^-n & k^\times \ar[r] & 0 } \]
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}(X) \ar[r]^-n & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r] & 0. } \]
In addition, we have an exact follow-up:
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}^0(X) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}(X) \ar[r]^-{\deg} & \mathbb{Z} \ar[r] & 0 , } \]

and \(\operatorname{Pic}^0(X)\) is identified with the group of rational points on \(k\) of an abelian variety of size \(g\), the jacobian of \(X\). In such a group, the multiplication by \(n\) is overjective and its kernel is a \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-free module of rank \(2 g\) (because \(n\) is invertible in \(k\)); hence the corollary.
From clever unscrewing, using the "track method," it is possible to obtain in corollary the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3.6 ([2])

Be \(k\) an algebraically closed field, \(X\) an algebraic curve on \(k\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). So:

  1. We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(q>2\).

  2. If \(X\) is affine, we even have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(q>1\).
For the demonstration, as well as for the presentation of the "track method," we refer to [2].

To calculate the cohomology of size varieties \(>1\) fibrations are used by curves, which allows us to reduce ourselves to studying morphisms whose fibers are of dimension \(\leqslant 1\). This principle has several variants, let's say a few.

Be \(A\) a \(k\)-algebra of finite type and \(a_1,\dotsc,a_n\) generators of \(A\). If you place \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(X_i=\operatorname{Spec}(k[a_1,\dotsc,a_i])\), \(X_n=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the canonical inclusions \(k[a_1,\dotsc,a_i]\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k[a_1,\dotsc,a_i,a_{i+1}]\) define morphisms \(X_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{n-1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_0\) whose fibres are of size \(\leqslant 1\).

In the case of smooth morphism, we can be more precise. elemental fibration a schematic morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) that can be immersed in a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j \ar[dr]_-f & \overline{X} \ar[d]^-{\overline{f}} & Y \ar@{_{(}->}[l]_-i \ar[dl]^-g\\ & S } \]
meeting the following conditions:

1. \(j\) is a dense open immersion in each fiber and \(X=\overline{X} \setminus Y\).

  2. \(\overline{f}\) is smooth and projective, with irreducible geometrical fibres and dimensions \(1\).

  3. \(g\) is a surface and no fibres of \(g\) are empty.

We're calling. Good neighborhood concerning \(S\) a \(S\)-scheme \(X\) as it exists \(S\)-schemes \(X=X_n,\dotsc,X_0=S\) and elementary fibrations \(f_i:X_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{i-1}\), \(i=1,\dotsc,n\). [2] that if \(X\) is a smooth pattern on an algebraically closed field \(k\) any rational point of \(X\) has an open neighborhood which is a good neighborhood (relating to \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\)).
One can unscrew a proper morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in the following way. According to Chow's lemma, there is a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[dr]_-f & \overline{X} \ar[l]_-\pi \ar[d]^-{\overline{f}}\\ & S } \]

where \(\pi\) and \(\overline{f}\) are projective morphisms, \(\pi\) being in addition an isomorphism above a dense open \(X\). Locally on \(S\), \(\overline{X}\) is a closed sub-scheme of a projective space type \(\mathbb{P}_S^n\).

The latter is unscrewed by considering the projection \(\varphi:\mathbb{P}_S^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbb{P}_S^1\) which sends the point of homogenous coordinates \((x_0,x_1,\dotsc,x_n)\) on \((x_0,x_1)\). It is a rational application defined outside the closed \(Y\simeq \mathbb{P}_S^{n-2}\) of \(\mathbb{P}_S^n\) of homogeneous equations \(x_0=x_1=0\). Either \(u:P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{P}_S^n\) the center burst \(Y\); the fibres of \(u\) either of size \(\leqslant 1\). Moreover, there is a natural morphism \(v:P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{P}_S^1\) which prolongs the rational application \(\varphi\) and \(v\) makes \(P\) a \(\mathbb{P}_S^1\)-scheme locally isomorphic to the projective space type \(\mathbb{P}^{n-1}\) which can be projected on \(\mathbb{P}^1\), etc.

One can sweep a projective and smooth variety \(X\) by a Lefschetz brush. The burst \(\widetilde X\) of the intersection of the brush axis with \(X\) is projected on \(\mathbb{P}^1\) and the fibers of this projection are the hyperplane sections of \(X\) by the hyperplanes of the brush.

pages.title: Theorem of basic change for a proper morphism
pages.html:
4 Theorem of basic change for a proper morphism

4.1 Introduction

This chapter is devoted to the demonstration and applications of the

Theorem 4.1.1

Been \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism of diagrams and \(\mathscr{F}\) an abelian sheaf of torsion on \(X\). So, whatever it is \(q\geqslant 0\), the fibre of \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) in a geometric point \(s\) of the \(S\) is isomorphic to cohomology \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathscr{F})\) Of fibre \(X_s=X\otimes_S \operatorname{Spec} k(s)\) of the \(f\) in \(s\).

For \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a clean and separate continuous application (separated means that the diagonal of \(X\times_S X\) is closed) between topological spaces, and \(\mathscr{F}\) an abelian sheaf on \(X\), the similar result is well known, and elementary: as \(f\) is closed, the \(f^{-1}(V)\) for \(V\) Neighborhood \(s\) are a fundamental system of neighbourhoods of \(X_s\), and we're checking that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathscr{F}) = \varinjlim_U \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(U,\mathscr{F})\), for \(U\) travelling through the neighbourhoods of \(X_s\)In practice, \(X_s\) has even a fundamental system \(\mathscr{U}\) Neighborhoods \(U\) of which it is retracted by deformation and, for \(\mathscr{F}\) constant, so we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathscr{F})=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(U,\mathscr{F})\). In imaginative terms: the special fiber swallows the general fiber.

In the case of diagrams the demonstration is more delicate and it is essential to assume that \(\mathscr{F}\) is torsional ([2]) Taking into account the description of the fibres of \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) (2.3.3), theorem (4.1.1) is essentially equivalent to

Theorem 4.1.2

Be \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian and \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism and \(X_0\) the closed fiber of \(f\). So, for any torsion abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) and for all \(q\geqslant 0\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathscr{F})\).

By going to the limit one can see that it is enough to demonstrate the theorem when \(A\) is the strict heselized of a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra of the finite type into a first ideal. First we deal with the case \(q=0\) or \(1\) and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\) (4.2) An argument based on the concept of a constructible sheaf (4.3) shows that it is sufficient to consider the case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is constant.3.4.3) makes it possible to assume that \(X_0\) is a curve; in this case it is only necessary to demonstrate the theorem for \(q=2\) (4.4).

Among other applications (4.6), the theorem allows to define the concept of self-supporting cohomology (4.5).

4.2 Demonstration for \(q=0\) or \(1\) and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\)

The result for \(q=0\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) constant is equivalent to the following proposal (Zariski connection theorem):

Proposition 4.2.1

Be \(A\) a local Noetheran Henselian ring and \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism and \(X_0\) the closed fiber of \(f\). Then the related component sets \(\pi_0(X)\) and \(\pi_0(X_0)\) are in bijection.

It is up to the same to show that the sets of parts both open and closed \(\operatorname{Of}(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Of}(X_0)\) are in bijection. It is known that the set \(\operatorname{Of}(X)\) corresponds bijectively to the set of idempotents of \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\), likewise \(\operatorname{Of}(X_0)\) corresponds bijectively to the set of idempotents of \(\Gamma(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0})\). It is therefore a question of showing that the canonical application

\[ \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0}) \]

It's bijective.

Note \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximum ideal of \(A\), \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)^\wedge\) the completed \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\) for topology \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic and, for the whole \(n\geqslant 0\), \(X_n=X\otimes_A A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\). Based on the theorem of finiteness for own morphisms [20], \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\) is a \(A\)-finished algebra; as \(A\) is a Henselian, the result is that the canonical application

\[ \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)^\wedge \]

is bijective. In particular the canonical application

\[ \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)^\wedge \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X_n,\mathscr{O}_{X_n}) \]

is bijective. But, since \(X_n\) and \(X_0\) on the same underlying topological space, the canonical application

\[ \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X_n,\mathscr{O}_{X_n}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0}) \]

is bijective for everything \(n\), which ends the demonstration.

Since \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is in bijection with all the isomorphism classes of galoisian floor coverings of \(X\) group \(\mathbb{Z}/n\), the theorem for \(q=1\) and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\) results from the following proposal.

Proposition 4.2.2

Be \(A\) a local Noetheran Henselian ring and \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism and \(X_0\) the closed fiber of \(f\). Then the restriction searcher

\[ \operatorname{Rev.et}(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Rev.et}(X_0) \]

is an equivalence of categories.

(If \(X_0\) is related and if a geometric point of \(X_0\) has been chosen as the base point, it is tantamount to saying that the canonical application \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) on the fundamental (profinite) groups is bijective).

The proposal (4.2.1) shows that this actuator is fully faithful. Indeed, if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two floor coverings of \(X\), a \(X\)-morphism of \(X'\) in \(X''\) is determined by its graph which is an open and closed part of \(X'\times_X X''\).

It is therefore a question of showing that any span coating \(X_0'\) of \(X_0\) extends into a span coating of \(X\). It is known that the span coatings do not depend on the nilpotent elements [23], therefore \(X_0'\) stands up in a unique way in a spreading coating \(X_n'\) of \(X_n\) for all \(n\geqslant 0\), i.e. in a spreading coating \(\mathfrak{X}'\) of the formal scheme \(\mathfrak{X}\) completed with \(X\) long of \(X_0\). Based on the theorem of algebraization of the formal coherent sheaves of Grothendieck (existence theorem, [20]), \(\mathfrak{X}'\) is the formal completion of a span coating \(\overline{X}'\), of \(\overline{X}=X\otimes_A \widehat{A}\).

By going to the limit, it is sufficient to demonstrate the proposal in case \(A\) is the hesalized of a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra of finite type. You can then apply Artin's approximation theorem to the spectator \(\mathscr{F}:(A \textnormal{-Algebras}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\textnormal{ensembles})\) which, at a \(A\)-Algebra \(B\), matches all the isomorphism classes of floor coverings of \(X\otimes_A B\). Indeed, this searcher is locally of finite presentation: if \(B_i\) is an inductive filtering system of \(A\)-algebras and if \(B=\varinjlim B_i\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(B) = \varinjlim \mathscr{F}(B_i)\). According to Artin's theorem, given an element \(\xi\in \mathscr{F}(\widehat{A})\), in this case the isomorphism class of \(\overline{X}'\), there is \(\xi\in \mathscr{F}(A)\) having the same image as \(\overline{\xi}\) in \(\mathscr{F}(A/\mathfrak{m})\). In other words, there is a surface \(X'\) of the \(X\) of which the restriction to \(X_0\) is isomorphic to \(X_0'\).

4.3. constructible sheaves

In this paragraph, a diagram is considered. New Zealand \(X\) and the sheaf is called \(X\) a sheaf Abelian on \({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\).

Definition 4.3.1

It is said that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) is locally constant constructible (in abbreviated l.c.) if it is represented by a span coating of \(X\).

Definition 4.3.2

It is said that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) is constructible if it checks the following equivalent conditions:

  1. There is a superjective finite family of sub-schemes \(X_i\) from \(X\) such as the restriction from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(X_i\) i.e. l.c.

  2. There is a finite family of finite morphisms \(p_i:X_i'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), for each \(i\) a constant constructible sheaf (= defined by a finite abelian group) \(C_i\) on \(X_i'\), and a monomorphism \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \prod p_{i*} C_i\).

It is easily verified that the category of constructible sheaves on \(X\) is an abelian category. Furthermore, if \(u:\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) is a sheaf homomorphism and if \(\mathscr{F}\) is constructible, the sheaf \(\operatorname{im}(u)\) is constructible.

Lemma 4.3.3

Any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) is an inductive filter limit of constructible sheaves.

In fact, if \(j:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a finite spatial pattern on \(X\), an element \(\xi\in \mathscr{F}(U)\) such that \(n\xi=0\) defines a sheaf homomorphism \(j_! \underline{\mathbb{Z}/n}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) whose image (the small subbeam of \(\mathscr{F}\) of which \(\xi\) is a local section) is a constructible subbeam of \(\mathscr{F}\). It is clear that \(\mathscr{F}\) is an inductive limit of such subbeams.

Definition 4.3.4

Be \(\mathscr{C}\) an abelian category and \(T\) a searcher defined on \(\mathscr{C}\) at values in the category of abelian groups. It will be said that \(T\) is Eraseable in \(\mathscr{C}\) if, for any object \(A\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and all \(\alpha\in T(A)\), there is a monomorphism \(u:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\) in \(\mathscr{C}\) such as \(T(u)\alpha = 0\).

Lemma 4.3.5

The \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,-)\) for \(q>0\) are eraseable in the category of constructible sheaves on \(X\).

It is sufficient to note that, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a constructible sheaf, there necessarily exists an integer \(n>0\) such that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules. Then there is a monomorphism \(\mathscr{F}\hookrightarrow \mathscr{G}\), where \(\mathscr{G}\) is a sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{G}) = 0\) for all \(q>0\). For example, we can take for \(\mathscr{G}\) the Godement resolution \(\prod_{x\in X} i_{x*} \mathscr{F}_{\overline{x}}\), where \(x\) runs through the dots of \(X\) and \(i_x:\overline{x}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a geometric point centred in \(X\).4.3.3) \(\mathscr{G}\) is inductive limit of constructible sheaves, from where the lemma, because the actuators \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,-)\) switch to inductive limits.

Lemma 4.3.6

Either \(\varphi^\bullet:T^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {T'}^\bullet\) a morphism of cohomological characters defined on an abelian category \(\mathscr{C}\) and with values in the abelian group category. Suppose that \(T^q\) is eraseable for \(q>0\) and either \(\mathscr{E}\) a subset of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) such that any object of \(\mathscr{C}\) is contained in an object belonging to \(\mathscr{E}\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\varphi^q(A)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and everything \(A\in\operatorname{Ob} \mathscr{C}\).

  2. \(\varphi^0(M)\) is bijective and \(\varphi^q(M)\) overjective for all \(q>0\) and all \(M\in\mathscr{E}\).

  3. \(\varphi^0(A)\) is bijective for everything \(A\in\operatorname{Ob} \mathscr{C}\) and \({T'}^q\) is eraseable for everything \(q>0\).

The demonstration is done by recurrence on \(q\) and does not present any difficulties.

Proposition 4.3.7

Be \(X_0\) a sub-scheme of \(X\). Suppose that, for everything \(n\geqslant 0\) and for any scheme \(X'\) finished on \(X\), the canonical application

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X',\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0',\mathbb{Z}/n)\text{,} \]

where \(X_0' = X'\times_X X_0\), is bijective for \(q=0\) and overjective for \(q>0\). So, for any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) and for all \(q\geqslant 0\), the canonical application

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathscr{F}) \]

It's bijective.

By going to the limit, just demonstrate the assertion for \(\mathscr{F}\) constructible. Apply the lemma (4.3.6) by taking for \(\mathscr{C}\) the category of constructible sheaves on \(X\), \(T^q=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,-)\), \({T'}^q=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,-)\), and \(\mathscr{E}\) the set of constructible sheaves of the shape \(\prod p_{i*} C_i\), where \(p_i:X_i'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a finished morphism and \(C_i\) a constant finite sheaf on \(X_i'\).

4.4 End of demonstration

By the method of fibration by curves (3.4.3), we simply demonstrate the theorem in relative dimension \(\leqslant 1\). According to the previous paragraph, it will suffice to show that, if \(S\) is the spectrum of a local noetherian ring strictly Henselia, \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism whose closed fiber \(X_0\) is of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and \(n\) an integer \(\geqslant 0\), canonical homomorphism

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/n) \]

is bijective for \(q=0\) and surjective for \(q>0\).

The cases \(q=0\) and \(1\) have been seen above and we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q\geqslant 3\); therefore we only have to deal with the case \(q=2\). Of course we can assume that \(n\) is a power of a prime number. If \(n=p^r\), where \(p\) is the characteristic of the residual field of \(S\), the theory of Artin-Schreier shows that a \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/p^r) = 0\). If \(n=\ell^r\), \(\ell\ne p\), a commutative diagram is derived from the theory of Kummer

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Pic}(X) \ar[d] \ar[r]^-\alpha & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^r) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Pic}(X_0) \ar[r]^-\beta & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^r) } \]

where the application \(\beta\) is surjective (It has been seen in the chapter 3 for a smooth curve on an algebraically closed field, but similar arguments apply to any curve on a separably closed field).

To conclude, it will be sufficient to show:

Proposition 4.4.1

Be \(S\) the spectrum of a local Henselian Noetherian ring and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism whose closed fiber \(X_0\) is of size \(\leqslant 1\). Then the canonical application of restriction

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X_0) \]

is surjective (It is enough that the morphism \(f\) is separated of finite type).

To simplify the demonstration, we will assume that \(X\) is integrity, although this is not necessary. Any invertible sheaf on \(X_0\) is associated with a Cartier divisor (because \(X_0\) is a curve, so almost projective), it is sufficient to show that the canonical application \(\operatorname{Div}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Div}(X_0)\) is overjective.

Any divisor on \(X_0\) is a linear combination of divisors whose support is concentrated in a single closed, uninsulated point of \(X_0\). Be \(x\) such a point, \(t_0\in\mathscr{O}_{X_0,x}\) a regular, non-invertible component of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_0,x}\) and \(D_0\) the divisor concentrated in \(x\) Local Equation \(t_0\). Either \(U\) an open neighbourhood of \(x\) in \(X\) as there is a section \(t\in \Gamma(U,\mathscr{O}_U)\) of which: \(t_0\). Either \(Y\) the closed \(U\) Equation \(t=0\); even to be taken \(U\) small enough, we can assume that \(x\) is the only point of \(Y\cap X_0\). Then \(Y\) is near-finished above \(S\) in \(x\); since \(S\) is the spectrum of a local Henselian ring, it is deduced that \(Y=Y_1\amalg Y_2\), where \(Y_1\) is finished on \(S\) and where \(Y_2\) does not meet \(X_0\). Moreover, as \(X\) is separated on \(S\), \(Y_1\) is closed in \(X\).

If you want to replace \(U\) with a smaller open neighbourhood of \(x\), you can assume that \(Y=Y_1\), i.e. \(Y\) is closed in \(X\). You then define a divisor \(D\) on \(X\) relevant \(D_0\) by posing \(D|X\setminus Y=0\) and \(D|U=\operatorname{div}(t)\) what makes sense because \(t\) is invertible on \(U\setminus Y\).

4.4.2. Note

In the case where \(f\) is clean, one could also demonstrate the same style as the proposal (4.2.2) Indeed, since \(X_0\) is a curve, there is no obstruction to raise an invertible sheaf on \(X_0\) in infinitesimal neighborhoods \(X_n\) of \(X_0\), therefore to the formal completion \(\mathfrak{X}\) of \(X\) along \(X_0\). We then conclude by successively applying Grothendieck's theorem of existence and Artin's approximation theorem.

4.5 Properly supported cohomology

Definition 4.5.1

Either \(X\) a separate scheme of finite type on a field \(k\). According to a Nagata theorem, there is a diagram \(\overline{X}\) clean on \(k\) and an open immersion \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{X}\). For any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) we note \(j_! \mathscr{F}\) the extension by \(0\) from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(\overline{X}\) and we define the groups of self-supporting cohomology

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X,\mathscr{F})=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\overline{X}, j_! \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

Let's show that this definition is independent of the compactification \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{X}\) chosen. Be \(j_1:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}_1\) and \(j_2:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}_2\) two compactifications. So \(X\) is sent to \(\overline{X}_1\times \overline{X}_2\) by \(x\mapsto (j_1(x),j_2(x))\) and the image closed \(\overline{X}_3\) by \(X\) by this application is a compactification of \(X\). So we have a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ & \overline{X}_1 \\ X \ar[ur]^-{j_1} \ar[rr]^-{(j_1,j_2)} \ar[dr]_-{j_2} & & \overline{X}_3 \ar[ul]_-{p_1} \ar[dl]^-{p_2} \\ & \overline{X}_2 } \]

where \(p_1\) and \(p_2\), restrictions of natural projections to \(\overline{X}_3\), are clean morphisms. Therefore, it is sufficient to treat the case where one has a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{j_2} \ar[dr]_-{j_1} & \overline{X}_2 \ar[d]^-p \\ & \overline{X}_1 } \]

with \(p\) a proper morphism.

Lemma 4.5.2

We have \(p_*(j_{2!} \mathscr{F}) = j_{1!} \mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathsf{R}^q p_*(j_{2!} \mathscr{F}) = 0\), for \(q>0\).

Let's note right away that lemma is enough to conclude. Using the Leray spectral sequence of morphism \(p\), we deduce that we have, for everything \(q\geqslant 0\),

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\overline{X}_2, j_{2!} \mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\overline{X}_1, j_{1!} \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

To demonstrate the lemma, fiber by fiber is reasond using the basic change theorem (4.1.1) for \(p\). The result is immediate, because, over a point of \(X\), \(p\) is an isomorph and, over a point of \(\overline{X}_1\setminus X\), \(j_{2!} \mathscr{F}\) is null on the fibre of \(p\).

4.5.3

Similarly, if \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a separated morphism of a finite type of noetherian schematic, there is a proper morphism \(\overline{f}:\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and an open immersion \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}\). The upper direct images with compact support are then defined \(\mathsf{R}^q f_!\) by posing for any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\)

\[ \mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F} = \mathsf{R}^q f_*(j_! \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

It is verified as before that this definition is independent of the compactification chosen.

Theorem 4.5.4

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of the finite type of noetherian diagrams and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). Then the fiber of \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\) at a geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) is is isomorphic to the compact support cohomology \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X_s,\mathscr{F})\) of the fibre \(X_s\) of \(f\) in \(s\).

It is a simple variant of the basic change theorem for a proper morphism (4.1.1) More generally, if

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]^-f & X' \ar[l]_-{g'} \ar[d]^-{f'} \\ S & S' \ar[l]^-g } \]

is a Cartesian diagram, we have a canonical isomorph

\[ \tag{4.5.4.1} g^* (\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}) \simeq \mathsf{R}^q f_!'({g'}^* \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

4.6 Applications

Theorem 4.6.1 (cancellation)

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of finite type whose fibres are of size \(\leqslant n\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). So we have \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F} = 0\) for \(q>2 n\).

According to the basic change theorem, it can be assumed that \(S\) is the spectrum of a separably enclosed field. If \(\dim X = n\), there is an open affin \(U\) of \(X\) such as \(\dim(X\setminus U) < n\); then there is an exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_U \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_{X\setminus U} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) and, by recurrence on \(n\), it is sufficient to demonstrate the theorem for \(X=U\) affine. Then the fibration method by curves (3.4.1) and the basic change theorem allow to be reduced to a curve on a separably enclosed field for which the desired result of Tsen's theorem is deduced (3.3.6).

Theorem 4.6.2 (fineness)

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of finite type and \(\mathscr{F}\) a sheaf constructible on \(X\). Then the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\) are constructible.

We only consider the case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is cancelled by an invertible integer on \(X\).

By demonstrating the theorem one goes back to the case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is a constant sheaf \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}/n}\) and where \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a clean and smooth morphism whose fibres are geometrically connected curves of genus \(g\). For \(n\) invertible on \(X\), the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) are then locally free of finished row, void for \(q>2\) (4.6.1) Replacement \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) by the locally isomorphic sheaf (on \(S\)) \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), canonically

\[ \tag{4.6.2.1} \begin{aligned} \mathsf{R}^0 f_* \boldsymbol{\mu}_n &= \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \\ \mathsf{R}^1 f_* \boldsymbol{\mu}_n &= \underline{\operatorname{Pic}}(X/S)_n \\ \mathsf{R}^2 f_* \boldsymbol{\mu}_n &= \mathbb{Z}/n \text{.} \end{aligned} \]
Theorem 4.6.3 (comparison with classical cohomology)

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of the finite type diagrams on \(\mathbb{C}\), and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). Note by an exponent \({(-)}^{\mathrm{an}}\) the passing oscillator to the usual topological spaces, and by \(\mathsf{R}^q {f}^{\mathrm{an}}_!\) the oscillating oscillators derived from the compact support direct image oscillator by \({f}^{\mathrm{an}}\).

\[ {(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F})}^{\mathrm{an}} \simeq \mathsf{R}^q {f}^{\mathrm{an}}_! {\mathscr{F}}^{\mathrm{an}} \text{.} \]

In particular, for \(S=\) a point and \(\mathscr{F}\) the constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}/n\),

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q({X}^{\mathrm{an}},\mathbb{Z}/n)\text{.} \]

Unscrews using the bottom change theorem bring us back to the case where \(X\) is a clean and smooth curve, where \(S=\) a point, and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\). The cohomology groups considered are then null for \(q\ne 0,1,2\), and we invoke [32]: indeed, if \(X\) is clean on \(\mathbb{C}\), we have \(\pi_0(X) = \pi_0({X}^{\mathrm{an}})\) and \(\pi_1(X) = \) completed profinite of \(\pi_1({X}^{\mathrm{an}})\), hence the assertion for \(q=0,1\). For \(q=2\), we use the exact sequence of Kummer and the fact that, by [32] again, \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=\operatorname{Pic}({X}^{\mathrm{an}})\).

Theorem 4.6.4 (cohomological dimension of affin schemes)

Be \(X\) a finished affine pattern on a separably enclosed field and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). So we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(q>\dim(X)\).

For the very nice demonstration we are going back to [2], XIV §2 and 3.

4.6.5 Note

This theorem is in a way a substitute for Morse theory. Let us consider in fact the classic case where \(X\) is smooth and affine on \(\mathbb{C}\) immersed in an affine space type \(\mathbb{C}^N\). So, for almost any point \(p\in\mathbb{C}^N\), the function "distance to \(p\)" on \(X\) is a function of Morse and the indices of its critical points are smaller than \(\dim(X)\). Thus \(X\) is obtained by recollation of index loops smaller than \(\dim(X)\), hence the classical analogue of (4.6.4).

blocks.html: 4 Theorem of basic change for a proper morphism
4.1 Introduction
This chapter is devoted to the demonstration and applications of the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1.1

Been \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism of diagrams and \(\mathscr{F}\) an abelian sheaf of torsion on \(X\). So, whatever it is \(q\geqslant 0\), the fibre of \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) in a geometric point \(s\) of the \(S\) is isomorphic to cohomology \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathscr{F})\) Of fibre \(X_s=X\otimes_S \operatorname{Spec} k(s)\) of the \(f\) in \(s\).

For \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a clean and separate continuous application (separated means that the diagonal of \(X\times_S X\) is closed) between topological spaces, and \(\mathscr{F}\) an abelian sheaf on \(X\), the similar result is well known, and elementary: as \(f\) is closed, the \(f^{-1}(V)\) for \(V\) Neighborhood \(s\) are a fundamental system of neighbourhoods of \(X_s\), and we're checking that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathscr{F}) = \varinjlim_U \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(U,\mathscr{F})\), for \(U\) travelling through the neighbourhoods of \(X_s\)In practice, \(X_s\) has even a fundamental system \(\mathscr{U}\) Neighborhoods \(U\) of which it is retracted by deformation and, for \(\mathscr{F}\) constant, so we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathscr{F})=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(U,\mathscr{F})\). In imaginative terms: the special fiber swallows the general fiber.

In the case of diagrams the demonstration is more delicate and it is essential to assume that \(\mathscr{F}\) is torsional ([2]) Taking into account the description of the fibres of \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) (2.3.3), theorem (4.1.1) is essentially equivalent to

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1.2

Be \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian and \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism and \(X_0\) the closed fiber of \(f\). So, for any torsion abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) and for all \(q\geqslant 0\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathscr{F})\).

By going to the limit one can see that it is enough to demonstrate the theorem when \(A\) is the strict heselized of a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra of the finite type into a first ideal. First we deal with the case \(q=0\) or \(1\) and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\) (4.2) An argument based on the concept of a constructible sheaf (4.3) shows that it is sufficient to consider the case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is constant.3.4.3) makes it possible to assume that \(X_0\) is a curve; in this case it is only necessary to demonstrate the theorem for \(q=2\) (4.4).
Among other applications (4.6), the theorem allows to define the concept of self-supporting cohomology (4.5).
4.2 Demonstration for \(q=0\) or \(1\) and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\)
The result for \(q=0\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) constant is equivalent to the following proposal (Zariski connection theorem):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2.1

Be \(A\) a local Noetheran Henselian ring and \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism and \(X_0\) the closed fiber of \(f\). Then the related component sets \(\pi_0(X)\) and \(\pi_0(X_0)\) are in bijection.

It is up to the same to show that the sets of parts both open and closed \(\operatorname{Of}(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Of}(X_0)\) are in bijection. It is known that the set \(\operatorname{Of}(X)\) corresponds bijectively to the set of idempotents of \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\), likewise \(\operatorname{Of}(X_0)\) corresponds bijectively to the set of idempotents of \(\Gamma(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0})\). It is therefore a question of showing that the canonical application
\[ \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0}) \]
It's bijective.

Note \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximum ideal of \(A\), \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)^\wedge\) the completed \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\) for topology \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic and, for the whole \(n\geqslant 0\), \(X_n=X\otimes_A A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\). Based on the theorem of finiteness for own morphisms [20], \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\) is a \(A\)-finished algebra; as \(A\) is a Henselian, the result is that the canonical application
\[ \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)^\wedge \]
is bijective. In particular the canonical application
\[ \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)^\wedge \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X_n,\mathscr{O}_{X_n}) \]
is bijective. But, since \(X_n\) and \(X_0\) on the same underlying topological space, the canonical application
\[ \operatorname{Idem}\Gamma(X_n,\mathscr{O}_{X_n}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Idem} \Gamma(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0}) \]
is bijective for everything \(n\), which ends the demonstration.

Since \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is in bijection with all the isomorphism classes of galoisian floor coverings of \(X\) group \(\mathbb{Z}/n\), the theorem for \(q=1\) and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\) results from the following proposal.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2.2

Be \(A\) a local Noetheran Henselian ring and \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism and \(X_0\) the closed fiber of \(f\). Then the restriction searcher

\[ \operatorname{Rev.et}(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Rev.et}(X_0) \]

is an equivalence of categories.

(If \(X_0\) is related and if a geometric point of \(X_0\) has been chosen as the base point, it is tantamount to saying that the canonical application \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) on the fundamental (profinite) groups is bijective).

The proposal (4.2.1) shows that this actuator is fully faithful. Indeed, if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two floor coverings of \(X\), a \(X\)-morphism of \(X'\) in \(X''\) is determined by its graph which is an open and closed part of \(X'\times_X X''\).

It is therefore a question of showing that any span coating \(X_0'\) of \(X_0\) extends into a span coating of \(X\). It is known that the span coatings do not depend on the nilpotent elements [23], therefore \(X_0'\) stands up in a unique way in a spreading coating \(X_n'\) of \(X_n\) for all \(n\geqslant 0\), i.e. in a spreading coating \(\mathfrak{X}'\) of the formal scheme \(\mathfrak{X}\) completed with \(X\) long of \(X_0\). Based on the theorem of algebraization of the formal coherent sheaves of Grothendieck (existence theorem, [20]), \(\mathfrak{X}'\) is the formal completion of a span coating \(\overline{X}'\), of \(\overline{X}=X\otimes_A \widehat{A}\).

By going to the limit, it is sufficient to demonstrate the proposal in case \(A\) is the hesalized of a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra of finite type. You can then apply Artin's approximation theorem to the spectator \(\mathscr{F}:(A \textnormal{-Algebras}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\textnormal{ensembles})\) which, at a \(A\)-Algebra \(B\), matches all the isomorphism classes of floor coverings of \(X\otimes_A B\). Indeed, this searcher is locally of finite presentation: if \(B_i\) is an inductive filtering system of \(A\)-algebras and if \(B=\varinjlim B_i\), we have \(\mathscr{F}(B) = \varinjlim \mathscr{F}(B_i)\). According to Artin's theorem, given an element \(\xi\in \mathscr{F}(\widehat{A})\), in this case the isomorphism class of \(\overline{X}'\), there is \(\xi\in \mathscr{F}(A)\) having the same image as \(\overline{\xi}\) in \(\mathscr{F}(A/\mathfrak{m})\). In other words, there is a surface \(X'\) of the \(X\) of which the restriction to \(X_0\) is isomorphic to \(X_0'\).
4.3. constructible sheaves
In this paragraph, a diagram is considered. New Zealand \(X\) and the sheaf is called \(X\) a sheaf Abelian on \({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.1

It is said that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) is locally constant constructible (in abbreviated l.c.) if it is represented by a span coating of \(X\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.2

It is said that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) is constructible if it checks the following equivalent conditions:

  1. There is a superjective finite family of sub-schemes \(X_i\) from \(X\) such as the restriction from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(X_i\) i.e. l.c.

  2. There is a finite family of finite morphisms \(p_i:X_i'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), for each \(i\) a constant constructible sheaf (= defined by a finite abelian group) \(C_i\) on \(X_i'\), and a monomorphism \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \prod p_{i*} C_i\).

It is easily verified that the category of constructible sheaves on \(X\) is an abelian category. Furthermore, if \(u:\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) is a sheaf homomorphism and if \(\mathscr{F}\) is constructible, the sheaf \(\operatorname{im}(u)\) is constructible.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3.3

Any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) is an inductive filter limit of constructible sheaves.

In fact, if \(j:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a finite spatial pattern on \(X\), an element \(\xi\in \mathscr{F}(U)\) such that \(n\xi=0\) defines a sheaf homomorphism \(j_! \underline{\mathbb{Z}/n}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) whose image (the small subbeam of \(\mathscr{F}\) of which \(\xi\) is a local section) is a constructible subbeam of \(\mathscr{F}\). It is clear that \(\mathscr{F}\) is an inductive limit of such subbeams.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.4

Be \(\mathscr{C}\) an abelian category and \(T\) a searcher defined on \(\mathscr{C}\) at values in the category of abelian groups. It will be said that \(T\) is Eraseable in \(\mathscr{C}\) if, for any object \(A\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and all \(\alpha\in T(A)\), there is a monomorphism \(u:A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\) in \(\mathscr{C}\) such as \(T(u)\alpha = 0\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3.5

The \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,-)\) for \(q>0\) are eraseable in the category of constructible sheaves on \(X\).

It is sufficient to note that, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a constructible sheaf, there necessarily exists an integer \(n>0\) such that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules. Then there is a monomorphism \(\mathscr{F}\hookrightarrow \mathscr{G}\), where \(\mathscr{G}\) is a sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{G}) = 0\) for all \(q>0\). For example, we can take for \(\mathscr{G}\) the Godement resolution \(\prod_{x\in X} i_{x*} \mathscr{F}_{\overline{x}}\), where \(x\) runs through the dots of \(X\) and \(i_x:\overline{x}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a geometric point centred in \(X\).4.3.3) \(\mathscr{G}\) is inductive limit of constructible sheaves, from where the lemma, because the actuators \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,-)\) switch to inductive limits.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3.6

Either \(\varphi^\bullet:T^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {T'}^\bullet\) a morphism of cohomological characters defined on an abelian category \(\mathscr{C}\) and with values in the abelian group category. Suppose that \(T^q\) is eraseable for \(q>0\) and either \(\mathscr{E}\) a subset of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) such that any object of \(\mathscr{C}\) is contained in an object belonging to \(\mathscr{E}\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\varphi^q(A)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and everything \(A\in\operatorname{Ob} \mathscr{C}\).

  2. \(\varphi^0(M)\) is bijective and \(\varphi^q(M)\) overjective for all \(q>0\) and all \(M\in\mathscr{E}\).

  3. \(\varphi^0(A)\) is bijective for everything \(A\in\operatorname{Ob} \mathscr{C}\) and \({T'}^q\) is eraseable for everything \(q>0\).
The demonstration is done by recurrence on \(q\) and does not present any difficulties.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.7

Be \(X_0\) a sub-scheme of \(X\). Suppose that, for everything \(n\geqslant 0\) and for any scheme \(X'\) finished on \(X\), the canonical application

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X',\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0',\mathbb{Z}/n)\text{,} \]

where \(X_0' = X'\times_X X_0\), is bijective for \(q=0\) and overjective for \(q>0\). So, for any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) and for all \(q\geqslant 0\), the canonical application

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathscr{F}) \]

It's bijective.

By going to the limit, just demonstrate the assertion for \(\mathscr{F}\) constructible. Apply the lemma (4.3.6) by taking for \(\mathscr{C}\) the category of constructible sheaves on \(X\), \(T^q=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,-)\), \({T'}^q=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,-)\), and \(\mathscr{E}\) the set of constructible sheaves of the shape \(\prod p_{i*} C_i\), where \(p_i:X_i'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a finished morphism and \(C_i\) a constant finite sheaf on \(X_i'\).
4.4 End of demonstration

By the method of fibration by curves (3.4.3), we simply demonstrate the theorem in relative dimension \(\leqslant 1\). According to the previous paragraph, it will suffice to show that, if \(S\) is the spectrum of a local noetherian ring strictly Henselia, \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism whose closed fiber \(X_0\) is of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and \(n\) an integer \(\geqslant 0\), canonical homomorphism
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/n) \]
is bijective for \(q=0\) and surjective for \(q>0\).

The cases \(q=0\) and \(1\) have been seen above and we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q\geqslant 3\); therefore we only have to deal with the case \(q=2\). Of course we can assume that \(n\) is a power of a prime number. If \(n=p^r\), where \(p\) is the characteristic of the residual field of \(S\), the theory of Artin-Schreier shows that a \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/p^r) = 0\). If \(n=\ell^r\), \(\ell\ne p\), a commutative diagram is derived from the theory of Kummer

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Pic}(X) \ar[d] \ar[r]^-\alpha & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^r) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Pic}(X_0) \ar[r]^-\beta & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X_0,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^r) } \]

where the application \(\beta\) is surjective (It has been seen in the chapter 3 for a smooth curve on an algebraically closed field, but similar arguments apply to any curve on a separably closed field).
To conclude, it will be sufficient to show:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.1

Be \(S\) the spectrum of a local Henselian Noetherian ring and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism whose closed fiber \(X_0\) is of size \(\leqslant 1\). Then the canonical application of restriction

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(X_0) \]

is surjective (It is enough that the morphism \(f\) is separated of finite type).

To simplify the demonstration, we will assume that \(X\) is integrity, although this is not necessary. Any invertible sheaf on \(X_0\) is associated with a Cartier divisor (because \(X_0\) is a curve, so almost projective), it is sufficient to show that the canonical application \(\operatorname{Div}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Div}(X_0)\) is overjective.

Any divisor on \(X_0\) is a linear combination of divisors whose support is concentrated in a single closed, uninsulated point of \(X_0\). Be \(x\) such a point, \(t_0\in\mathscr{O}_{X_0,x}\) a regular, non-invertible component of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_0,x}\) and \(D_0\) the divisor concentrated in \(x\) Local Equation \(t_0\). Either \(U\) an open neighbourhood of \(x\) in \(X\) as there is a section \(t\in \Gamma(U,\mathscr{O}_U)\) of which: \(t_0\). Either \(Y\) the closed \(U\) Equation \(t=0\); even to be taken \(U\) small enough, we can assume that \(x\) is the only point of \(Y\cap X_0\). Then \(Y\) is near-finished above \(S\) in \(x\); since \(S\) is the spectrum of a local Henselian ring, it is deduced that \(Y=Y_1\amalg Y_2\), where \(Y_1\) is finished on \(S\) and where \(Y_2\) does not meet \(X_0\). Moreover, as \(X\) is separated on \(S\), \(Y_1\) is closed in \(X\).

If you want to replace \(U\) with a smaller open neighbourhood of \(x\), you can assume that \(Y=Y_1\), i.e. \(Y\) is closed in \(X\). You then define a divisor \(D\) on \(X\) relevant \(D_0\) by posing \(D|X\setminus Y=0\) and \(D|U=\operatorname{div}(t)\) what makes sense because \(t\) is invertible on \(U\setminus Y\).

In the case where \(f\) is clean, one could also demonstrate the same style as the proposal (4.2.2) Indeed, since \(X_0\) is a curve, there is no obstruction to raise an invertible sheaf on \(X_0\) in infinitesimal neighborhoods \(X_n\) of \(X_0\), therefore to the formal completion \(\mathfrak{X}\) of \(X\) along \(X_0\). We then conclude by successively applying Grothendieck's theorem of existence and Artin's approximation theorem.
4.5 Properly supported cohomology

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.5.1

Either \(X\) a separate scheme of finite type on a field \(k\). According to a Nagata theorem, there is a diagram \(\overline{X}\) clean on \(k\) and an open immersion \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{X}\). For any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) we note \(j_! \mathscr{F}\) the extension by \(0\) from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(\overline{X}\) and we define the groups of self-supporting cohomology

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X,\mathscr{F})=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\overline{X}, j_! \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

Let's show that this definition is independent of the compactification \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{X}\) chosen. Be \(j_1:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}_1\) and \(j_2:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}_2\) two compactifications. So \(X\) is sent to \(\overline{X}_1\times \overline{X}_2\) by \(x\mapsto (j_1(x),j_2(x))\) and the image closed \(\overline{X}_3\) by \(X\) by this application is a compactification of \(X\). So we have a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ & \overline{X}_1 \\ X \ar[ur]^-{j_1} \ar[rr]^-{(j_1,j_2)} \ar[dr]_-{j_2} & & \overline{X}_3 \ar[ul]_-{p_1} \ar[dl]^-{p_2} \\ & \overline{X}_2 } \]
where \(p_1\) and \(p_2\), restrictions of natural projections to \(\overline{X}_3\), are clean morphisms. Therefore, it is sufficient to treat the case where one has a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{j_2} \ar[dr]_-{j_1} & \overline{X}_2 \ar[d]^-p \\ & \overline{X}_1 } \]
with \(p\) a proper morphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5.2

We have \(p_*(j_{2!} \mathscr{F}) = j_{1!} \mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathsf{R}^q p_*(j_{2!} \mathscr{F}) = 0\), for \(q>0\).
Let's note right away that lemma is enough to conclude. Using the Leray spectral sequence of morphism \(p\), we deduce that we have, for everything \(q\geqslant 0\),
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\overline{X}_2, j_{2!} \mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\overline{X}_1, j_{1!} \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

To demonstrate the lemma, fiber by fiber is reasond using the basic change theorem (4.1.1) for \(p\). The result is immediate, because, over a point of \(X\), \(p\) is an isomorph and, over a point of \(\overline{X}_1\setminus X\), \(j_{2!} \mathscr{F}\) is null on the fibre of \(p\).

Similarly, if \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a separated morphism of a finite type of noetherian schematic, there is a proper morphism \(\overline{f}:\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and an open immersion \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}\). The upper direct images with compact support are then defined \(\mathsf{R}^q f_!\) by posing for any torsion sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\)
\[ \mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F} = \mathsf{R}^q f_*(j_! \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]
It is verified as before that this definition is independent of the compactification chosen.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.5.4

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of the finite type of noetherian diagrams and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). Then the fiber of \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\) at a geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) is is isomorphic to the compact support cohomology \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X_s,\mathscr{F})\) of the fibre \(X_s\) of \(f\) in \(s\).
It is a simple variant of the basic change theorem for a proper morphism (4.1.1) More generally, if
\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]^-f & X' \ar[l]_-{g'} \ar[d]^-{f'} \\ S & S' \ar[l]^-g } \]
is a Cartesian diagram, we have a canonical isomorph
\[ \tag{4.5.4.1} g^* (\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}) \simeq \mathsf{R}^q f_!'({g'}^* \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]
4.6 Applications

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
