Theorem 4.6.1 (cancellation)

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of finite type whose fibres are of size \(\leqslant n\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). So we have \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F} = 0\) for \(q>2 n\).

According to the basic change theorem, it can be assumed that \(S\) is the spectrum of a separably enclosed field. If \(\dim X = n\), there is an open affin \(U\) of \(X\) such as \(\dim(X\setminus U) < n\); then there is an exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_U \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_{X\setminus U} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) and, by recurrence on \(n\), it is sufficient to demonstrate the theorem for \(X=U\) affine. Then the fibration method by curves (3.4.1) and the basic change theorem allow to be reduced to a curve on a separably enclosed field for which the desired result of Tsen's theorem is deduced (3.3.6).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6.2 (fineness)

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of finite type and \(\mathscr{F}\) a sheaf constructible on \(X\). Then the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F}\) are constructible.
We only consider the case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is cancelled by an invertible integer on \(X\).

By demonstrating the theorem one goes back to the case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is a constant sheaf \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}/n}\) and where \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a clean and smooth morphism whose fibres are geometrically connected curves of genus \(g\). For \(n\) invertible on \(X\), the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) are then locally free of finished row, void for \(q>2\) (4.6.1) Replacement \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) by the locally isomorphic sheaf (on \(S\)) \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), canonically

\[ \tag{4.6.2.1} \begin{aligned} \mathsf{R}^0 f_* \boldsymbol{\mu}_n &= \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \\ \mathsf{R}^1 f_* \boldsymbol{\mu}_n &= \underline{\operatorname{Pic}}(X/S)_n \\ \mathsf{R}^2 f_* \boldsymbol{\mu}_n &= \mathbb{Z}/n \text{.} \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6.3 (comparison with classical cohomology)

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of the finite type diagrams on \(\mathbb{C}\), and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). Note by an exponent \({(-)}^{\mathrm{an}}\) the passing oscillator to the usual topological spaces, and by \(\mathsf{R}^q {f}^{\mathrm{an}}_!\) the oscillating oscillators derived from the compact support direct image oscillator by \({f}^{\mathrm{an}}\).

\[ {(\mathsf{R}^q f_! \mathscr{F})}^{\mathrm{an}} \simeq \mathsf{R}^q {f}^{\mathrm{an}}_! {\mathscr{F}}^{\mathrm{an}} \text{.} \]

In particular, for \(S=\) a point and \(\mathscr{F}\) the constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}/n\),

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^q({X}^{\mathrm{an}},\mathbb{Z}/n)\text{.} \]

Unscrews using the bottom change theorem bring us back to the case where \(X\) is a clean and smooth curve, where \(S=\) a point, and \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\). The cohomology groups considered are then null for \(q\ne 0,1,2\), and we invoke [32]: indeed, if \(X\) is clean on \(\mathbb{C}\), we have \(\pi_0(X) = \pi_0({X}^{\mathrm{an}})\) and \(\pi_1(X) = \) completed profinite of \(\pi_1({X}^{\mathrm{an}})\), hence the assertion for \(q=0,1\). For \(q=2\), we use the exact sequence of Kummer and the fact that, by [32] again, \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=\operatorname{Pic}({X}^{\mathrm{an}})\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6.4 (cohomological dimension of affin schemes)

Be \(X\) a finished affine pattern on a separably enclosed field and \(\mathscr{F}\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). So we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F}) = 0\) for \(q>\dim(X)\).
For the very nice demonstration we are going back to [2], XIV §2 and 3.

This theorem is in a way a substitute for Morse theory. Let us consider in fact the classic case where \(X\) is smooth and affine on \(\mathbb{C}\) immersed in an affine space type \(\mathbb{C}^N\). So, for almost any point \(p\in\mathbb{C}^N\), the function "distance to \(p\)" on \(X\) is a function of Morse and the indices of its critical points are smaller than \(\dim(X)\). Thus \(X\) is obtained by recollation of index loops smaller than \(\dim(X)\), hence the classical analogue of (4.6.4).

pages.title: Local cycling of smooth morphisms
pages.html:
5 Local cycling of smooth morphisms

Been \(X\) a complex analytical variety and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} D\) a morphism of \(X\) It's on the record. \([0,t]\) the closed right end segment \(0\) and \(t\) in \(D\) and \((0,t]\) the semi-open segment. \(f\) is smooth, inclusion

\[ j : f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right) \]

is an equivalent of homotopy; can grow special fiber \(X_0=f^{-1}(0)\) in \(f^{-1}([0,t])\).

In practice, for \(t\) rather small, \(f^{-1}((0,t])\) will be a fibrous on \((0,t]\) so that inclusion

\[ X_t = f^{-1}(t) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right) \]

will also be an equivalency of homotopy. morphism of co-specialization the homotopy class of applications:

\[ \operatorname{cosp} : X_0 \hookrightarrow f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right) \xleftarrow{\sim} f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right) \xleftarrow{\sim} X_t \text{.} \]

One can express this construction in imaginative terms by saying that, for a smooth morphism, the general fiber swallows the special fiber.

Let's not assume \(f\) necessarily smooth (but suppose that \(f^{-1}((0,t])\) is a fibrated on \((0,t]\)). We can still define a morphism \(\operatorname{cosp}^\bullet\) in cohomology as soon as \(j_* \mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z} = 0\) for \(q>0\). Under these assumptions, Leray's spectral sequence for \(j\) shows that we have

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \]

and \(\operatorname{cosp}^\bullet\) is the morphism composed of:

\[ \operatorname{cosp}^\bullet : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}) \xleftarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \xleftarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_0,\mathbb{Z}) \]

The fiber of \(\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z}\) in one point \(x\in X_0\) is calculated as follows. A ball is taken in an ambient space \(B_\varepsilon\) of center \(x\) and radius \(\varepsilon\) rather small, and for \(\eta\) rather small, one lays \(E=X\cap B_\varepsilon \cap f^{-1}(\eta t)\); it is the variety of evanescent cycles in \(x\).

\[ (\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z})_x \xleftarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q\left(X\cap B_\varepsilon\cap f^{-1}\left((0,\eta t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(E,\mathbb{Z}) \]

and co-specialization morphism is defined in cohomology as the varieties of evanescent cycles are acyclic (\(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(E,\mathbb{Z})=\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(E,\mathbb{Z}) = 0\) for \(q>0\)), which is expressed by saying that \(f\) is locally acyclic.

This chapter is devoted to the analogy of this situation for a smooth morphism of patterns for the spreading cohomology. However, it is essential in this context to limit itself to the coefficients of torsion and order first to residual characteristicsThe paragraph 5.1 is dedicated to generalities on locally acyclic morphisms and co-specialization arrows. 5.2, a smooth morphism is shown to be locally acyclic. 5.3, this result is combined with those of the previous chapter to deduce two applications: a theorem of specialization of the cohomology groups (the cohomology of geometric fibres of a clean and smooth morphism is locally constant) and a theorem of basic change by a smooth morphism.

In all that follows, one fixes an integer \(n\) and "scheme" means "scheme on which \(n\) is invertible." "geometric point" will always mean "algebraic geometric point" (2.3.1) \(x:\operatorname{Spec}(k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), with \(k\) algebraically closed.

5.1 Locally acyclic morphisms

5.1.1 Rating

Given a diagram \(S\) and a geometric point \(s\) of \(S\), we will note \(\widetilde S^s\) the spectrum of the strict localization of \(S\) in \(s\).

Definition 5.1.2

It is said that a geometric point \(t\) of \(S\) is a generation of \(s\) if it is defined by an algebraic fencing of the residual field of a \(\widetilde S^s\). It is also said that \(s\) is a specialization of \(t\) and it is called specialisation arrow \(S\)-morphism \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\).

Definition 5.1.3

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a diagram morphism. Be \(s\) a geometric point of \(S\), \(t\) a generization of \(s\), \(x\) a geometric point of \(X\) above \(s\) and \(\widetilde X_t^x = \widetilde X^x\times_{\widetilde S^s} t\). So it is said that \(\widetilde X_t^x\) is a variety of evanescent cycles from \(f\) to \(x\).

It is said that \(f\) is locally acyclic if, the reduced cohomology of any variety of evanescent cycles \(\widetilde X_t^x\) is nil:

\[ \tag{5.1.3.1} \widehat{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^\bullet(\widetilde X_t^x,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\text{,} \]

i.e. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\left(X_t^x,\mathbb{Z}/n\right) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\widetilde X_t^x,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\).

Lemma 5.1.4

Been \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) locally acyclic morphism and \(g:S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a near-finished morphism (or projective limit of near-finished morphisms). \(f':X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) deducted from \(f\) by basic change is locally acyclic.

It is verified that any variety of evanescent cycles of \(f'\) is a variety of evanescent cycles of \(f\).

Lemma 5.1.5

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a locally acyclic morphism. For any geometric point \(t\) of \(S\), giving rise to a Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X_t \ar[r]^-{\varepsilon'} \ar[d] & X \ar[d]^-f \\ t \ar[r]^-\varepsilon & S } \]

We have \(\varepsilon_*' \mathbb{Z}/n = f^*\varepsilon_* \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_*'\mathbb{Z}/n=0\) for \(q>0\).

Be \(\overline{S}\) the adhesion of \(\varepsilon(t)\), \(S'\) the standard of \(\overline{S}\) in \(k(t)\), and the Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X_t \ar[r]^-{f'} \ar[d] & X' \ar[r]^-{\alpha'} \ar[d]^-{f'} & X \ar[d]^-f \\ t \ar[r]^-i & S' \ar[r]^-\alpha & S } \]

The local rings of \(S'\) are normal with fields of fractions separably enclosed. They are therefore strictly Henselian, and the local acyclicity of \(f'\) (5.1.4) provides \(f_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n\), \(\mathsf{R}^qi_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\). Since \(\alpha\) is whole, then we have

\[ \mathsf{R}^q\varepsilon_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = \alpha_*' \mathsf{R}^q i_*' \mathbb{Z}/n = \alpha_*' {f'}^* \mathsf{R}^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/n = f^* \alpha_* \mathsf{R}^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/n = f^* \mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_* \mathbb{Z}/n \text{,} \]

and the lemma.

5.1.6

Given locally acyclic morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and a specialisation arrow \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\), we will define canonical homomorphisms, known as cospecialization arrows

\[ \operatorname{cosp}^\bullet : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\text{,} \]

connecting the cohomology of the general fibre \(X_t=X\times_S t\) and that of the special fibre \(X_s=X\times_X s\).

Consider the Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X_t \ar[d] \ar[r]^-{\varepsilon'} & \widetilde X \ar[d]^-{f'} & X_s \ar[d] \ar[l]\\ t \ar[r]^-\varepsilon & \widetilde S^s & s \ar[l]} \]

deducted from \(f\) by change of base.5.1.4), \(f'\) is still locally acyclic. From the definition of local acyclicity, the restriction to \(X_s\) of sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_*' \mathbb{Z}/n\) is \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) for \(q=0\), and \(0\) for \(q>0\). By (5.1.5) we even know that \(\mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\). We define \(\operatorname{cosp}^\bullet\) as the compound arrow

\[ \tag{5.1.6.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\varepsilon_*' \mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n) \text{.} \]

Variant

Be \(\overline{S}\) the adhesion of \(\varepsilon(t)\) in \(\widetilde S^s\), \(S'\) the standard of \(\overline{S}\) in \(k(t)\) and \(X'/S'\) deducted from \(X/S\) by change of base.5.1.6.1) can still be written

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X',\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n) \text{.} \]
Theorem 5.1.7

Be \(S\) a locally noetherian scheme, \(s\) a geometric point of \(S\) and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism.

  1. morphism \(f\) is locally acyclic,

  2. for any specialisation morphism \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\) and for all \(q\geqslant 0\), the cospecialization arrows \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) are bijective.

Then canonical homomorphism \((\mathsf{R}^q f_*\mathbb{Z}/n)_s\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\).

To demonstrate the theorem, it is clear that one can assume \(S=\widetilde S^s\). One goes and makes show that, for any sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-modules \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(S\), canonical homomorphism \(\varphi^q(\mathscr{F}):(\mathsf{R}^q f_* f^* \mathscr{F})_s \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,f^*\mathscr{F})\) is bijective.

Any sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) is an inductive filter limit of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-modules (4.3.3In addition, any constructible sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-modules is plunged into a sheaf of the shape \(\prod i_{\lambda *} C_\lambda\), where \(i_\lambda:t_\lambda\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a finite family of generations of \(s\) and \(C_\lambda\) a \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-free module finished on \(t_\lambda\). According to the definition of the co-specialization arrows, condition (b) means that the homomorphisms \(\varphi^q(\mathscr{F})\) are bijective if \(\mathscr{F}\) is of this form.

It is concluded by means of a variation of the lemma (4.3.6):

Lemma 5.1.8

Either \(\mathscr{C}\) an abelian category in which the filtering inductive limits exist. Either \(\varphi^\bullet:T^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{T'}^\bullet\) a morphism of cohomological functioners switching to the filtering inductive limits, defined on \(\mathscr{C}\) and with values in the category of abelian groups. Suppose there are two sub-assemblies \(\mathscr{D}\) and \(\mathscr{E}\) of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) such as:

  1. any object of \(\mathscr{C}\) is an inductive filter limit of objects belonging to \(\mathscr{D}\),

  2. any object belonging to \(\mathscr{D}\) is contained in an object belonging to \(\mathscr{E}\).

Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\varphi^q(A)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and everything \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{C}\).

  2. \(\varphi^q(M)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and everything \(M\in\mathscr{E}\).

The demonstration of the lemma is done by passing to the inductive limit, recurrence on \(q\) and repeated application of the lemma of the five to the exact sequence diagram of cohomology deduced from an exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), with \(A\in\mathscr{D}\), \(M\in\mathscr{E}\), \(A'\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{C}\).

Corollary 5.1.9

Been \(S\) the spectrum of a local Noetherian ring strictly Henselian and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a locally acyclic morphism. Suppose that, for any geometric point \(t\) of the \(S\) they say \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\) (i.e. geometrical fibers of \(f\) then we've got \(f_* \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\mathsf{R}^qf_*\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\).

Corollary 5.1.10

Been \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and \(g:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) morphisms of locally noetherian patterns. So, if \(f\) and \(g\) are locally acyclic, the same is true of \(g\circ f\).

It can be assumed that \(X\), \(Y\) and \(Z\) are strictly local and that \(f\) and \(g\) are local morphisms. It is then a question of showing that, if \(z\) is an algebraic geometric point of \(Z\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\).

Since \(g\) is locally acyclic, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(Y_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(Y_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\). Moreover, morphism \(f_z:X_z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_z\) is locally acyclic (5.1.4) and its geometrical fibres are acyclic, as they are varieties of evanescent cycles of \(f\).5.1.9), we have \(\mathsf{R}^q {f_z}_*\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\). Moreover \({f_z}_*\mathbb{Z}/n\) is constant of fiber \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) on \(Y_z\). We conclude with the help of the spectral sequence of Leray of \(f_z\).

5.2 Local cycling of smooth morphism

Theorem 5.2.1

A smooth morphism is locally acyclic.

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a smooth morphism. The assertion is local for the spreading topology on \(X\) and \(S\), it can therefore be assumed that \(X\) is the affin space type of dimension \(d\) on \(S\). By passing to the limit, it can be assumed that \(S\) is noetherian and the transitivity of local acyclicity (5.1.10) shows that it is sufficient to process the case \(d=1\).

Been \(s\) a geometric point of \(S\) and \(x\) a geometric point of \(X\) centered in a closed point of \(X_s\). It is a question of showing that the geometric fibers of morphism \(\widetilde X^x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\) We'll be laying down from now on. \(S=\widetilde S^s=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=\widetilde X^x\). We have \(X\simeq \operatorname{Spec} A\{T\}\), where \(A\{T\}\) is the Heselisis of \(A[T]\) at point \(T=0\) above \(s\).

If \(t\) is a geometric point of \(S\), the fiber \(X_t\) is projective limit of affines and smooth curves on \(t\). Therefore, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q\geqslant 0\) and we only have to show that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(n\) first to the residual characteristic of \(S\). This results from the following two proposals.

Proposition 5.2.2

Be \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian, \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\{T\}\). Then the geometric fibers of \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) are related.

We can go back to limit in case \(A\) is a strict henselized of a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra of finite type.

Been \(\overline{t}\) a geometric point of \(S\), located in \(t\), and \(k'\) a finite extension separable from \(k(t)\) in \(k(\overline{t})\)We're laying down. \(t'=\operatorname{Spec}(k')\) and \(X_{t'} = X\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(k')\). We have to check that, whatever is \(\overline{t}\) and \(t'\), \(X_{t'}\) is related (meaning related and not empty). \(A'\) the standardized \(A\) in \(k'\), i.e. the ring of the elements of \(k'\) integers on the image of \(A\) in \(k(t)\). We have \(A\{t\}\otimes_A A'\xrightarrow{\sim} A'\{T\}\): the left-wing member is indeed henselian local ( \(A'\) is finished on \(A\), and local) and limit of local algebras on \(A'\{T\}=A\{T\}\otimes_A A'\). The diagram \(X_{t'}\) is therefore still the fiber in \(t'\) of the \(X=\operatorname{Spec}\left(A'\{T\}\right)\) on \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\). The local scheme \(X'\) is normal, therefore integrity; localized sound \(X_{t'}\) is still integrity, a fortiori related.

Proposition 5.2.3

Been \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian, \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=\operatorname{Spec}\left(A\{T\}\right)\). Be \(\overline{t}\) a geometric point of \(S\) and \(X_{\overline{t}}\) the corresponding geometric fiber. Then any Galoisian spreading coating of \(X_{\overline{t}}\) of order first to the characteristic of the residual field of \(A\) It's trivial.

Lemma 5.2.4 (Zariski-Nagata purity theorem in size \(2\))

Be \(C\) a regular local ring of size \(2\) and \(C'\) a \(C\)-normal finite algebra spread above the additional opening of the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(C)\). So \(C'\) is spread over \(C\).

Indeed \(C'\) is normal in size \(2\), therefore \(\operatorname{prof}(C')=2\). Since \(\operatorname{prof}(C')+\dim\operatorname{proj}(C') = \dim(C)=2\), it is concluded that \(C'\) is free on \(C\). Then all the points of \(C\) where \(C'\) is branched is defined by an equation, the discriminant; since it does not contain a height point \(1\), it is empty.

Lemma 5.2.5 (special case of Abhyankar's lemma)

Be \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) a line, \(\pi\) a uniformizer, \(\eta\) the generic point of \(S\), \(X\) a smooth on \(S\), irreducible, of relative dimension \(1\), \(\widetilde X_\eta\) a Galoisian surface of \(X_\eta\), of degree \(n\) invertible on \(S\), and \(S_1=\operatorname{Spec}\left(V[\pi^{1/n}]\right)\). Note by an index \((-)_1\) the basic change of \(S\) to \(S_1\). Then, \(\widetilde X_{1\eta}\) is extended into a spreading coating of \(X_1\).

This is \(\widetilde X_1\) the normalized \(X_1\) of \(\widetilde X_{1\eta}\). Given the structure of the moderate inertia groups of the discrete evaluation rings located from \(X\) at the generic points of the special fiber \(X_s\), \(\widetilde X_1\) is spread over \(X_1\) on the general fibre, and at the generic points of the special fibre.5.2.4), it's spread everywhere.

5.2.6

Note \(t\) the point in which \(\overline{t}\) is located. We are free to replace \(A\) with the normalized \(A\) in a finite extension that can be separated \(k(t')\) from \(k(t)\) in \(k(\overline{t})\) (cf. 5.2.2) This, and a passage to the preliminary limit, allow us to assume that

  1. \(A\) is normal noetherian, and \(t\) is the generic point of \(S\).

  2. The range coating considered to be \(X_{\overline{t}}\) comes from a spreading coating of \(X_t\).

  3. It comes from a spatial coating \(\widetilde X_U\) of the reciprocal image \(X_U\) of a non-empty open \(U\) of \(S\) (result of b): \(t\) is the limit of \(U\).

  4. The add-on to \(U\) is co-dimensional \(\geqslant 2\) (this is the price to enlarge \(k(t)\), by application of (5.2.5) to the discrete evaluation rings located from \(S\) in \(S\setminus U\) of co-dimensional \(1\) in \(S\); these dots are in finite number).

  5. The coating \(\widetilde X_U\) is trivial above the sub-scheme \(T=0\). (This leaves to enlarge \(k(t)\)).

When these conditions are met, we will see that the coating \(\widetilde X_U\) is trivial.

Lemma 5.2.7

Be \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian normal and Noetherian, \(U\) an open \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) whose complement is co-dimensional \(\geqslant 2\), \(V\) its reciprocal image in \(X=\operatorname{Spec}\left(A\{T\}\right)\) and \(V'\) a spatial coating of \(V\). If \(V'\) is trivial above \(T=0\), then \(V'\) is trivial.

Either \(B=\Gamma(V',\mathscr{O})\). Since \(V'\) is the inverse image of \(V\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\), you just have to see that \(B\) is finished spreading on \(A\{T\}\) (so decomposed, since \(A\{T\}\) is strictly Henselian). Or \(\widehat X=A[\![ T]\!]\)and note by \((-)^\wedge\) the basic change of \(X\) to \(\widehat X\). The diagram \(\widehat X\) is faithfully flat on \(X\). So we've got \(\Gamma(\widehat V',\mathscr{O}) = B\otimes_{A\{T\}} A[\![ T]\!]\), and just see that this ring \(\widehat B\) is finished spreading on \(A[\![ T]\!]\).

Either \(V_m\) (resp. \(V_m'\)) the sub-scheme of \(\widehat V\) (resp. \(\widehat V'\)) of equation \(T^{m+1} = 0\). By hypothesis, \(V_0'\) is a trivial coating of \(V_0\): a sum of \(n\) copies of \(V_0\). Similarly for \(V_m'/V_m\), since the floor coverings are insensitive to the nilipents.

\[ \varphi : \Gamma(\widehat V',\mathscr{O}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_m \Gamma(V_m',\mathscr{O}) = \left(\varprojlim_m \Gamma(V,\mathscr{O})\right)^n \text{.} \]

By hypothesis, the complement of \(U\) is of depth \(\geqslant 2\): on a \(\Gamma(V_m,\mathscr{O})=A[T]/(T^{m+1})\), and \(\varphi\) is a homomorphism of \(\widehat B\) in \(A[\![ T]\!]^n\). Above \(U\), it provides \(n\) separate sections of \(\widehat V'/\widehat V\): \(\widehat V'\) is trivial, sum of \(n\) copies of \(\widehat V\). The complement of \(\widehat V\) in \(\widehat X\) being still of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) (so depth \(\geqslant 2\)), it is inferred that \(\widehat B=A[\![ T]\!]^n\), hence the lemma.

5.3 Applications

Theorem 5.3.1 (specialization of cohomology groups)

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a clean and locally acyclic morphism, for example a clean and smooth morphism. Then the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q f_*\mathbb{Z}/n\) are locally constant constructible and for any specialisation arrow \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widetilde S^s\), the cospecialization arrows \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) are bijective.

This results immediately from the definition of the arrows of cospecialization and theorems of finiteness and basic change for the own.

Theorem 5.3.2 (basic change by smooth morphism)

Or a Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X' \ar[r]^-{g'} \ar[d]^-{f'} & X \ar[d]^-f \\ S' \ar[r]^-{g} & S } \]

with \(g\) smooth. For any sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\), torsion and first with residual characteristics of \(S\), we have

\[ g^* \mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{R}^q f_*'({g'}^* \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

By taking an open cover of \(X\)< one returns to the case where \(X\) is affine, then by a passage to the limit to box ì \(X\) is finished on \(S\). Then \(f\) becomes an open immersion \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}\) and a proper morphism \(\overline{f}:\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). From the spectral sequence of Leray for \(\overline{f}\circ j\) and from the basic theorem of change for clean morphisms, one deduces that it is sufficient to demonstrate theorem in case \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is an open immersion.

In this case, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is of the form \(\varepsilon_* C\), where \(\varepsilon:t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a geometric point of \(X\)< the theorem is corollary of (5.1.5) The general case results from this by the lemma (5.1.8).

Corollary 5.3.3

Be \(K/k\) a separably enclosed field extension, \(X\) a \(k\)-scheme and \(n\) a first integer to the characteristic of \(k\). Then the canonical application \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_K,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\).

Just note that \(\overline{K}\) is an inductive limit of \(\overline{k}\)- smooth algebras.

Theorem 5.3.4 (relative purity)

Or a commutative diagram

\[ \tag{5.3.4.1}\xymatrix{ U \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j \ar[dr] & X \ar[d]^-f & Y \ar[dl]^-h \ar@{_{(}->}[l]_-i\\ & S } \]

with \(f\) pure smooth of relative dimension \(N\), \(h\) pure smooth of relative dimension \(N-1\), \(i\) a closed dive and \(U=X\setminus Y\). For \(n\) first to the residual characteristics of \(S\), we have

\[ \begin{aligned} j_* \mathbb{Z}/n &= \mathbb{Z}/n \\ \mathsf{R}^1 j_*\mathbb{Z}/n &= \mathbb{Z}/n(-1)_Y \\ \mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n &= 0 \qquad \text{for } q\geqslant 2 \end{aligned} \]

In these formulas, \(\mathbb{Z}/n(-1)\) means the \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-duel of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\). If \(t\) is a local equation for \(Y\), the isomorphism \(\mathsf{R}^1j_*\mathbb{Z}/n\simeq\mathbb{Z}/n(-1)_Y\) is defined by the application \(a:\mathbb{Z}/n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}^1j_*\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) which sends \(1\) to the class of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\)-torseur of the roots \(n\)-ths of \(t\).

The question is local in nature. This allows to replace \((X,Y)\) by a locally isomorphic couple, for example

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathbb{A}_T^1 \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j \ar[dr]_-g & \mathbb{P}_T^1 \ar[d]^-f & T \ar[dl] \ar@{_{(}->}[l]_-i\\ & T } \]

with \(T=\mathbb{A}_S^{n-1}\) and \(i=\) infinity section. The corollary (5.1.9) applies to \(g\), and provides \(\mathsf{R}^q g_ \mathbb{Z}/n=\mathbb{Z}/n\) for \(q=0\), \(0\) for \(q>0\). For \(f\), we also have (4.6.2.1)

\[ \mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n,0,\mathbb{Z}/n(-1),0 \text{ for } q>2 \text{.} \]

It is easy to check that \(j_* \mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n\), and that \(\mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) are concentrated on \(i(T)\) for \(q>0\). The spectral sequence of Leray \(E_2^{pq} = \mathsf{R}^p f_* \mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n \Rightarrow \mathsf{R}^{p+q}g_* \mathbb{Z}/n\) is therefore reduced to

\[ \xymatrix{ i^* \mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/n & 0 & \cdots \\ i^* \mathsf{R}^1 j_* \mathbb{Z}/n \ar[drr]^-{d_2} & 0 & \cdots \\ \mathbb{Z}/n & 0 & \mathbb{Z}/n(-1) & 0 & \cdots } \]

\(\mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n=0\) for \(q\geqslant 0\), and \(\mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n\) is the zero extension of a locally free sheaf of rank one over \(T\) (isomorphic, via \(d_2\), at \(\mathbb{Z}/n(-1)\)). The application \(a\), defined above, being injective (as it is checked fiber by fiber), is isomorphism, and this proves (5.3.4).

5.3.5

We refer to [2] for the demonstration of the following applications of the acyclic theorem (5.2.1).

Theorem 5.3.6

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a finite type diagram morphism on \(\mathbb{C}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf on \(X\).

\[ {(\mathsf{R}^q f_*\mathscr{F})}^{\mathrm{an}} \sim \mathsf{R}^q {f}^{\mathrm{an}}_* ({\mathscr{F}}^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

(cf. 4.6.3: in ordinary cohomology, it is necessary to assume \(\mathscr{F}\) is constructible and not only of torsion).

Theorem 5.3.7

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a finite type diagram morphism on a field \(k\) a characteristic \(0\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf on \(X\). So, the \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) are also constructible.

The evidence uses the resolution of singularities and (5.3.4) It is widespread in the case of a finite type morphism of excellent patterns of character \(0\) in [2]. Another demonstration, independent of resolution, is given in this volume (VII, 1.1)

It applies to a morphism of finite type diagrams on a field or on a Dedekind ring.

blocks.html: 5 Local cycling of smooth morphisms

Been \(X\) a complex analytical variety and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} D\) a morphism of \(X\) It's on the record. \([0,t]\) the closed right end segment \(0\) and \(t\) in \(D\) and \((0,t]\) the semi-open segment. \(f\) is smooth, inclusion
\[ j : f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right) \]
is an equivalent of homotopy; can grow special fiber \(X_0=f^{-1}(0)\) in \(f^{-1}([0,t])\).
In practice, for \(t\) rather small, \(f^{-1}((0,t])\) will be a fibrous on \((0,t]\) so that inclusion
\[ X_t = f^{-1}(t) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right) \]
will also be an equivalency of homotopy. morphism of co-specialization the homotopy class of applications:
\[ \operatorname{cosp} : X_0 \hookrightarrow f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right) \xleftarrow{\sim} f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right) \xleftarrow{\sim} X_t \text{.} \]
One can express this construction in imaginative terms by saying that, for a smooth morphism, the general fiber swallows the special fiber.

Let's not assume \(f\) necessarily smooth (but suppose that \(f^{-1}((0,t])\) is a fibrated on \((0,t]\)). We can still define a morphism \(\operatorname{cosp}^\bullet\) in cohomology as soon as \(j_* \mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z} = 0\) for \(q>0\). Under these assumptions, Leray's spectral sequence for \(j\) shows that we have
\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \]
and \(\operatorname{cosp}^\bullet\) is the morphism composed of:

\[ \operatorname{cosp}^\bullet : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}) \xleftarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left((0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \xleftarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(f^{-1}\left([0,t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_0,\mathbb{Z}) \]

The fiber of \(\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z}\) in one point \(x\in X_0\) is calculated as follows. A ball is taken in an ambient space \(B_\varepsilon\) of center \(x\) and radius \(\varepsilon\) rather small, and for \(\eta\) rather small, one lays \(E=X\cap B_\varepsilon \cap f^{-1}(\eta t)\); it is the variety of evanescent cycles in \(x\).

\[ (\mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z})_x \xleftarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q\left(X\cap B_\varepsilon\cap f^{-1}\left((0,\eta t]\right),\mathbb{Z}\right) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(E,\mathbb{Z}) \]

and co-specialization morphism is defined in cohomology as the varieties of evanescent cycles are acyclic (\(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(E,\mathbb{Z})=\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(E,\mathbb{Z}) = 0\) for \(q>0\)), which is expressed by saying that \(f\) is locally acyclic.

This chapter is devoted to the analogy of this situation for a smooth morphism of patterns for the spreading cohomology. However, it is essential in this context to limit itself to the coefficients of torsion and order first to residual characteristicsThe paragraph 5.1 is dedicated to generalities on locally acyclic morphisms and co-specialization arrows. 5.2, a smooth morphism is shown to be locally acyclic. 5.3, this result is combined with those of the previous chapter to deduce two applications: a theorem of specialization of the cohomology groups (the cohomology of geometric fibres of a clean and smooth morphism is locally constant) and a theorem of basic change by a smooth morphism.

In all that follows, one fixes an integer \(n\) and "scheme" means "scheme on which \(n\) is invertible." "geometric point" will always mean "algebraic geometric point" (2.3.1) \(x:\operatorname{Spec}(k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), with \(k\) algebraically closed.
5.1 Locally acyclic morphisms
Given a diagram \(S\) and a geometric point \(s\) of \(S\), we will note \(\widetilde S^s\) the spectrum of the strict localization of \(S\) in \(s\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1.2

It is said that a geometric point \(t\) of \(S\) is a generation of \(s\) if it is defined by an algebraic fencing of the residual field of a \(\widetilde S^s\). It is also said that \(s\) is a specialization of \(t\) and it is called specialisation arrow \(S\)-morphism \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1.3

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a diagram morphism. Be \(s\) a geometric point of \(S\), \(t\) a generization of \(s\), \(x\) a geometric point of \(X\) above \(s\) and \(\widetilde X_t^x = \widetilde X^x\times_{\widetilde S^s} t\). So it is said that \(\widetilde X_t^x\) is a variety of evanescent cycles from \(f\) to \(x\).

It is said that \(f\) is locally acyclic if, the reduced cohomology of any variety of evanescent cycles \(\widetilde X_t^x\) is nil:

\[ \tag{5.1.3.1} \widehat{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^\bullet(\widetilde X_t^x,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\text{,} \]

i.e. \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\left(X_t^x,\mathbb{Z}/n\right) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(\widetilde X_t^x,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.1.4

Been \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) locally acyclic morphism and \(g:S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a near-finished morphism (or projective limit of near-finished morphisms). \(f':X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) deducted from \(f\) by basic change is locally acyclic.
It is verified that any variety of evanescent cycles of \(f'\) is a variety of evanescent cycles of \(f\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.1.5

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a locally acyclic morphism. For any geometric point \(t\) of \(S\), giving rise to a Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X_t \ar[r]^-{\varepsilon'} \ar[d] & X \ar[d]^-f \\ t \ar[r]^-\varepsilon & S } \]

We have \(\varepsilon_*' \mathbb{Z}/n = f^*\varepsilon_* \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_*'\mathbb{Z}/n=0\) for \(q>0\).
Be \(\overline{S}\) the adhesion of \(\varepsilon(t)\), \(S'\) the standard of \(\overline{S}\) in \(k(t)\), and the Cartesian diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ X_t \ar[r]^-{f'} \ar[d] & X' \ar[r]^-{\alpha'} \ar[d]^-{f'} & X \ar[d]^-f \\ t \ar[r]^-i & S' \ar[r]^-\alpha & S } \]

The local rings of \(S'\) are normal with fields of fractions separably enclosed. They are therefore strictly Henselian, and the local acyclicity of \(f'\) (5.1.4) provides \(f_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n\), \(\mathsf{R}^qi_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\). Since \(\alpha\) is whole, then we have

\[ \mathsf{R}^q\varepsilon_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = \alpha_*' \mathsf{R}^q i_*' \mathbb{Z}/n = \alpha_*' {f'}^* \mathsf{R}^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/n = f^* \alpha_* \mathsf{R}^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/n = f^* \mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_* \mathbb{Z}/n \text{,} \]
and the lemma.

Given locally acyclic morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and a specialisation arrow \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\), we will define canonical homomorphisms, known as cospecialization arrows

\[ \operatorname{cosp}^\bullet : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\text{,} \]
connecting the cohomology of the general fibre \(X_t=X\times_S t\) and that of the special fibre \(X_s=X\times_X s\).
Consider the Cartesian diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ X_t \ar[d] \ar[r]^-{\varepsilon'} & \widetilde X \ar[d]^-{f'} & X_s \ar[d] \ar[l]\\ t \ar[r]^-\varepsilon & \widetilde S^s & s \ar[l]} \]

deducted from \(f\) by change of base.5.1.4), \(f'\) is still locally acyclic. From the definition of local acyclicity, the restriction to \(X_s\) of sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_*' \mathbb{Z}/n\) is \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) for \(q=0\), and \(0\) for \(q>0\). By (5.1.5) we even know that \(\mathsf{R}^q \varepsilon_*'\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\). We define \(\operatorname{cosp}^\bullet\) as the compound arrow

\[ \tag{5.1.6.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\varepsilon_*' \mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n) \text{.} \]
Variant

Be \(\overline{S}\) the adhesion of \(\varepsilon(t)\) in \(\widetilde S^s\), \(S'\) the standard of \(\overline{S}\) in \(k(t)\) and \(X'/S'\) deducted from \(X/S\) by change of base.5.1.6.1) can still be written

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X',\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n) \text{.} \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1.7

Be \(S\) a locally noetherian scheme, \(s\) a geometric point of \(S\) and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism.

  1. morphism \(f\) is locally acyclic,

  2. for any specialisation morphism \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\) and for all \(q\geqslant 0\), the cospecialization arrows \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) are bijective.

Then canonical homomorphism \((\mathsf{R}^q f_*\mathbb{Z}/n)_s\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\).

To demonstrate the theorem, it is clear that one can assume \(S=\widetilde S^s\). One goes and makes show that, for any sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-modules \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(S\), canonical homomorphism \(\varphi^q(\mathscr{F}):(\mathsf{R}^q f_* f^* \mathscr{F})_s \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,f^*\mathscr{F})\) is bijective.

Any sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) is an inductive filter limit of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-modules (4.3.3In addition, any constructible sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-modules is plunged into a sheaf of the shape \(\prod i_{\lambda *} C_\lambda\), where \(i_\lambda:t_\lambda\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a finite family of generations of \(s\) and \(C_\lambda\) a \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-free module finished on \(t_\lambda\). According to the definition of the co-specialization arrows, condition (b) means that the homomorphisms \(\varphi^q(\mathscr{F})\) are bijective if \(\mathscr{F}\) is of this form.
It is concluded by means of a variation of the lemma (4.3.6):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.1.8

Either \(\mathscr{C}\) an abelian category in which the filtering inductive limits exist. Either \(\varphi^\bullet:T^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{T'}^\bullet\) a morphism of cohomological functioners switching to the filtering inductive limits, defined on \(\mathscr{C}\) and with values in the category of abelian groups. Suppose there are two sub-assemblies \(\mathscr{D}\) and \(\mathscr{E}\) of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) such as:

  1. any object of \(\mathscr{C}\) is an inductive filter limit of objects belonging to \(\mathscr{D}\),

  2. any object belonging to \(\mathscr{D}\) is contained in an object belonging to \(\mathscr{E}\).

Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\varphi^q(A)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and everything \(A\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{C}\).

  2. \(\varphi^q(M)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\) and everything \(M\in\mathscr{E}\).

The demonstration of the lemma is done by passing to the inductive limit, recurrence on \(q\) and repeated application of the lemma of the five to the exact sequence diagram of cohomology deduced from an exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), with \(A\in\mathscr{D}\), \(M\in\mathscr{E}\), \(A'\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{C}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.1.9

Been \(S\) the spectrum of a local Noetherian ring strictly Henselian and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a locally acyclic morphism. Suppose that, for any geometric point \(t\) of the \(S\) they say \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\) (i.e. geometrical fibers of \(f\) then we've got \(f_* \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\mathsf{R}^qf_*\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.1.10

Been \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and \(g:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) morphisms of locally noetherian patterns. So, if \(f\) and \(g\) are locally acyclic, the same is true of \(g\circ f\).

It can be assumed that \(X\), \(Y\) and \(Z\) are strictly local and that \(f\) and \(g\) are local morphisms. It is then a question of showing that, if \(z\) is an algebraic geometric point of \(Z\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\).

Since \(g\) is locally acyclic, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(Y_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\), and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(Y_z,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q>0\). Moreover, morphism \(f_z:X_z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_z\) is locally acyclic (5.1.4) and its geometrical fibres are acyclic, as they are varieties of evanescent cycles of \(f\).5.1.9), we have \(\mathsf{R}^q {f_z}_*\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(q>0\). Moreover \({f_z}_*\mathbb{Z}/n\) is constant of fiber \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) on \(Y_z\). We conclude with the help of the spectral sequence of Leray of \(f_z\).
5.2 Local cycling of smooth morphism

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2.1

A smooth morphism is locally acyclic.

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a smooth morphism. The assertion is local for the spreading topology on \(X\) and \(S\), it can therefore be assumed that \(X\) is the affin space type of dimension \(d\) on \(S\). By passing to the limit, it can be assumed that \(S\) is noetherian and the transitivity of local acyclicity (5.1.10) shows that it is sufficient to process the case \(d=1\).

Been \(s\) a geometric point of \(S\) and \(x\) a geometric point of \(X\) centered in a closed point of \(X_s\). It is a question of showing that the geometric fibers of morphism \(\widetilde X^x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde S^s\) We'll be laying down from now on. \(S=\widetilde S^s=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=\widetilde X^x\). We have \(X\simeq \operatorname{Spec} A\{T\}\), where \(A\{T\}\) is the Heselisis of \(A[T]\) at point \(T=0\) above \(s\).

If \(t\) is a geometric point of \(S\), the fiber \(X_t\) is projective limit of affines and smooth curves on \(t\). Therefore, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(q\geqslant 0\) and we only have to show that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(n\) first to the residual characteristic of \(S\). This results from the following two proposals.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.2

Be \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian, \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\{T\}\). Then the geometric fibers of \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) are related.
We can go back to limit in case \(A\) is a strict henselized of a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra of finite type.

Been \(\overline{t}\) a geometric point of \(S\), located in \(t\), and \(k'\) a finite extension separable from \(k(t)\) in \(k(\overline{t})\)We're laying down. \(t'=\operatorname{Spec}(k')\) and \(X_{t'} = X\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(k')\). We have to check that, whatever is \(\overline{t}\) and \(t'\), \(X_{t'}\) is related (meaning related and not empty). \(A'\) the standardized \(A\) in \(k'\), i.e. the ring of the elements of \(k'\) integers on the image of \(A\) in \(k(t)\). We have \(A\{t\}\otimes_A A'\xrightarrow{\sim} A'\{T\}\): the left-wing member is indeed henselian local ( \(A'\) is finished on \(A\), and local) and limit of local algebras on \(A'\{T\}=A\{T\}\otimes_A A'\). The diagram \(X_{t'}\) is therefore still the fiber in \(t'\) of the \(X=\operatorname{Spec}\left(A'\{T\}\right)\) on \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\). The local scheme \(X'\) is normal, therefore integrity; localized sound \(X_{t'}\) is still integrity, a fortiori related.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.3

Been \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian, \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=\operatorname{Spec}\left(A\{T\}\right)\). Be \(\overline{t}\) a geometric point of \(S\) and \(X_{\overline{t}}\) the corresponding geometric fiber. Then any Galoisian spreading coating of \(X_{\overline{t}}\) of order first to the characteristic of the residual field of \(A\) It's trivial.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.4 (Zariski-Nagata purity theorem in size \(2\))

Be \(C\) a regular local ring of size \(2\) and \(C'\) a \(C\)-normal finite algebra spread above the additional opening of the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(C)\). So \(C'\) is spread over \(C\).

Indeed \(C'\) is normal in size \(2\), therefore \(\operatorname{prof}(C')=2\). Since \(\operatorname{prof}(C')+\dim\operatorname{proj}(C') = \dim(C)=2\), it is concluded that \(C'\) is free on \(C\). Then all the points of \(C\) where \(C'\) is branched is defined by an equation, the discriminant; since it does not contain a height point \(1\), it is empty.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.5 (special case of Abhyankar's lemma)

Be \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) a line, \(\pi\) a uniformizer, \(\eta\) the generic point of \(S\), \(X\) a smooth on \(S\), irreducible, of relative dimension \(1\), \(\widetilde X_\eta\) a Galoisian surface of \(X_\eta\), of degree \(n\) invertible on \(S\), and \(S_1=\operatorname{Spec}\left(V[\pi^{1/n}]\right)\). Note by an index \((-)_1\) the basic change of \(S\) to \(S_1\). Then, \(\widetilde X_{1\eta}\) is extended into a spreading coating of \(X_1\).

This is \(\widetilde X_1\) the normalized \(X_1\) of \(\widetilde X_{1\eta}\). Given the structure of the moderate inertia groups of the discrete evaluation rings located from \(X\) at the generic points of the special fiber \(X_s\), \(\widetilde X_1\) is spread over \(X_1\) on the general fibre, and at the generic points of the special fibre.5.2.4), it's spread everywhere.

Note \(t\) the point in which \(\overline{t}\) is located. We are free to replace \(A\) with the normalized \(A\) in a finite extension that can be separated \(k(t')\) from \(k(t)\) in \(k(\overline{t})\) (cf. 5.2.2) This, and a passage to the preliminary limit, allow us to assume that

1. \(A\) is normal noetherian, and \(t\) is the generic point of \(S\).

  2. The range coating considered to be \(X_{\overline{t}}\) comes from a spreading coating of \(X_t\).

  3. It comes from a spatial coating \(\widetilde X_U\) of the reciprocal image \(X_U\) of a non-empty open \(U\) of \(S\) (result of b): \(t\) is the limit of \(U\).

  4. The add-on to \(U\) is co-dimensional \(\geqslant 2\) (this is the price to enlarge \(k(t)\), by application of (5.2.5) to the discrete evaluation rings located from \(S\) in \(S\setminus U\) of co-dimensional \(1\) in \(S\); these dots are in finite number).

  5. The coating \(\widetilde X_U\) is trivial above the sub-scheme \(T=0\). (This leaves to enlarge \(k(t)\)).
When these conditions are met, we will see that the coating \(\widetilde X_U\) is trivial.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.7

Be \(A\) a local ring strictly Henselian normal and Noetherian, \(U\) an open \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) whose complement is co-dimensional \(\geqslant 2\), \(V\) its reciprocal image in \(X=\operatorname{Spec}\left(A\{T\}\right)\) and \(V'\) a spatial coating of \(V\). If \(V'\) is trivial above \(T=0\), then \(V'\) is trivial.

Either \(B=\Gamma(V',\mathscr{O})\). Since \(V'\) is the inverse image of \(V\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\), you just have to see that \(B\) is finished spreading on \(A\{T\}\) (so decomposed, since \(A\{T\}\) is strictly Henselian). Or \(\widehat X=A[\![ T]\!]\)and note by \((-)^\wedge\) the basic change of \(X\) to \(\widehat X\). The diagram \(\widehat X\) is faithfully flat on \(X\). So we've got \(\Gamma(\widehat V',\mathscr{O}) = B\otimes_{A\{T\}} A[\![ T]\!]\), and just see that this ring \(\widehat B\) is finished spreading on \(A[\![ T]\!]\).

Either \(V_m\) (resp. \(V_m'\)) the sub-scheme of \(\widehat V\) (resp. \(\widehat V'\)) of equation \(T^{m+1} = 0\). By hypothesis, \(V_0'\) is a trivial coating of \(V_0\): a sum of \(n\) copies of \(V_0\). Similarly for \(V_m'/V_m\), since the floor coverings are insensitive to the nilipents.

\[ \varphi : \Gamma(\widehat V',\mathscr{O}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_m \Gamma(V_m',\mathscr{O}) = \left(\varprojlim_m \Gamma(V,\mathscr{O})\right)^n \text{.} \]

By hypothesis, the complement of \(U\) is of depth \(\geqslant 2\): on a \(\Gamma(V_m,\mathscr{O})=A[T]/(T^{m+1})\), and \(\varphi\) is a homomorphism of \(\widehat B\) in \(A[\![ T]\!]^n\). Above \(U\), it provides \(n\) separate sections of \(\widehat V'/\widehat V\): \(\widehat V'\) is trivial, sum of \(n\) copies of \(\widehat V\). The complement of \(\widehat V\) in \(\widehat X\) being still of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) (so depth \(\geqslant 2\)), it is inferred that \(\widehat B=A[\![ T]\!]^n\), hence the lemma.
5.3 Applications

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.1 (specialization of cohomology groups)

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a clean and locally acyclic morphism, for example a clean and smooth morphism. Then the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^q f_*\mathbb{Z}/n\) are locally constant constructible and for any specialisation arrow \(t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widetilde S^s\), the cospecialization arrows \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_t,\mathbb{Z}/n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_s,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) are bijective.
This results immediately from the definition of the arrows of cospecialization and theorems of finiteness and basic change for the own.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.2 (basic change by smooth morphism)

Or a Cartesian diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X' \ar[r]^-{g'} \ar[d]^-{f'} & X \ar[d]^-f \\ S' \ar[r]^-{g} & S } \]

with \(g\) smooth. For any sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\), torsion and first with residual characteristics of \(S\), we have

\[ g^* \mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{R}^q f_*'({g'}^* \mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

By taking an open cover of \(X\)< one returns to the case where \(X\) is affine, then by a passage to the limit to box ì \(X\) is finished on \(S\). Then \(f\) becomes an open immersion \(j:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{X}\) and a proper morphism \(\overline{f}:\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). From the spectral sequence of Leray for \(\overline{f}\circ j\) and from the basic theorem of change for clean morphisms, one deduces that it is sufficient to demonstrate theorem in case \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is an open immersion.

In this case, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is of the form \(\varepsilon_* C\), where \(\varepsilon:t\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a geometric point of \(X\)< the theorem is corollary of (5.1.5) The general case results from this by the lemma (5.1.8).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3.3

Be \(K/k\) a separably enclosed field extension, \(X\) a \(k\)-scheme and \(n\) a first integer to the characteristic of \(k\). Then the canonical application \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^q(X_K,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is bijective for everything \(q\geqslant 0\).
Just note that \(\overline{K}\) is an inductive limit of \(\overline{k}\)- smooth algebras.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.4 (relative purity)

Or a commutative diagram

\[ \tag{5.3.4.1}\xymatrix{ U \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j \ar[dr] & X \ar[d]^-f & Y \ar[dl]^-h \ar@{_{(}->}[l]_-i\\ & S } \]

with \(f\) pure smooth of relative dimension \(N\), \(h\) pure smooth of relative dimension \(N-1\), \(i\) a closed dive and \(U=X\setminus Y\). For \(n\) first to the residual characteristics of \(S\), we have

\[ \begin{aligned} j_* \mathbb{Z}/n &= \mathbb{Z}/n \\ \mathsf{R}^1 j_*\mathbb{Z}/n &= \mathbb{Z}/n(-1)_Y \\ \mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n &= 0 \qquad \text{for } q\geqslant 2 \end{aligned} \]

In these formulas, \(\mathbb{Z}/n(-1)\) means the \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-duel of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\). If \(t\) is a local equation for \(Y\), the isomorphism \(\mathsf{R}^1j_*\mathbb{Z}/n\simeq\mathbb{Z}/n(-1)_Y\) is defined by the application \(a:\mathbb{Z}/n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}^1j_*\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) which sends \(1\) to the class of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\)-torseur of the roots \(n\)-ths of \(t\).
The question is local in nature. This allows to replace \((X,Y)\) by a locally isomorphic couple, for example
\[ \xymatrix{ \mathbb{A}_T^1 \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j \ar[dr]_-g & \mathbb{P}_T^1 \ar[d]^-f & T \ar[dl] \ar@{_{(}->}[l]_-i\\ & T } \]

with \(T=\mathbb{A}_S^{n-1}\) and \(i=\) infinity section. The corollary (5.1.9) applies to \(g\), and provides \(\mathsf{R}^q g_ \mathbb{Z}/n=\mathbb{Z}/n\) for \(q=0\), \(0\) for \(q>0\). For \(f\), we also have (4.6.2.1)
\[ \mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n,0,\mathbb{Z}/n(-1),0 \text{ for } q>2 \text{.} \]

It is easy to check that \(j_* \mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n\), and that \(\mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) are concentrated on \(i(T)\) for \(q>0\). The spectral sequence of Leray \(E_2^{pq} = \mathsf{R}^p f_* \mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n \Rightarrow \mathsf{R}^{p+q}g_* \mathbb{Z}/n\) is therefore reduced to
\[ \xymatrix{ i^* \mathsf{R}^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/n & 0 & \cdots \\ i^* \mathsf{R}^1 j_* \mathbb{Z}/n \ar[drr]^-{d_2} & 0 & \cdots \\ \mathbb{Z}/n & 0 & \mathbb{Z}/n(-1) & 0 & \cdots } \]

\(\mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n=0\) for \(q\geqslant 0\), and \(\mathsf{R}^q j_*\mathbb{Z}/n\) is the zero extension of a locally free sheaf of rank one over \(T\) (isomorphic, via \(d_2\), at \(\mathbb{Z}/n(-1)\)). The application \(a\), defined above, being injective (as it is checked fiber by fiber), is isomorphism, and this proves (5.3.4).
We refer to [2] for the demonstration of the following applications of the acyclic theorem (5.2.1).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.6

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a finite type diagram morphism on \(\mathbb{C}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf on \(X\).

\[ {(\mathsf{R}^q f_*\mathscr{F})}^{\mathrm{an}} \sim \mathsf{R}^q {f}^{\mathrm{an}}_* ({\mathscr{F}}^{\mathrm{an}}) \]

(cf. 4.6.3: in ordinary cohomology, it is necessary to assume \(\mathscr{F}\) is constructible and not only of torsion).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.7

Be \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a finite type diagram morphism on a field \(k\) a characteristic \(0\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf on \(X\). So, the \(\mathsf{R}^q f_* \mathscr{F}\) are also constructible.

The evidence uses the resolution of singularities and (5.3.4) It is widespread in the case of a finite type morphism of excellent patterns of character \(0\) in [2]. Another demonstration, independent of resolution, is given in this volume (VII, 1.1)
It applies to a morphism of finite type diagrams on a field or on a Dedekind ring.

pages.title: Duality of Poincaré
pages.html:
Duality of Poincaré

6.1 Introduction

Either \(X\) an oriented topological variety, purely of size \(N\), and suppose that \(X\) admits a finished open cover \(\mathscr{U}=(U_i)_{1\leqslant i\leqslant K}\), such that the non-open intersections \(U_i\) are homeomorphic to balls. For such a variety, the duality theorem of Poincare can appear as follows:

  1. The cohomology of \(X\) is the cohomology of Čech corresponding to the coating \(\mathscr{U}\). This is the cohomology of the complex

    \[ \tag{1} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}^{A_0} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}^{A_1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \]

    where \(A_k=\{(i_0,\dotsc,i_k) : i_0<\cdots<i_k\text{ and } U_{i_0}\cap \cdots \cap U_{i_k} \ne \varnothing\}\).

  2. For \(a\in A_k\), \(a=(i_0,\dotsc,i_k)\), either \(U_a=U_{i_1}\cap \cdots \cap U_{i_k}\) or \(j_a\) the inclusion of \(U_a\) in \(X\). The constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}\) on \(X\) admits the resolution (left)

    \[ \tag{2} \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \displaystyle\bigoplus_{a\in A_1} j_{a!}\mathbb{Z} \ar[r] & \displaystyle\bigoplus_{a\in A_0} j_{a!} \mathbb{Z} \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & & \mathbb{Z} . } \]

    Compact support cohomology \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,j_{a!}\mathbb{Z})\) is the proper support cohomology of the ball (orientated) \(U_a\):

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X,j_{a!}\mathbb{Z}) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i\ne N \\ \mathbb{Z} & \text{if } i = N \end{cases} \]

The spectral sequence of hypercohomology, for the complex (2), and self-supporting cohomology, therefore states that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X)\) is the \((i-N)\)-th cohomology group of a complex

\[ \tag{3} \cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}^{A_1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}^{A_0} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \text{.} \]

This complex is the dual of the complex (1), hence the duality of Poincare.

The essential points of this construction are:

  1. the existence of a theory of self-supporting cohomology:

  2. the fact that any point \(x\) of a variety \(X\) of purely dimension \(N\) has a fundamental system of open neighborhoods \(U\) for which

    \[ \tag{4} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(U) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{for } i\ne N \text{,} \\ \mathbb{Z} & \text{for } i=N \text{.} \end{cases} \]

For \(X\) pure smooth size \(N\) on \(k\) algebraically closed, \(n\) invertible on \(X\) and \(x\) a closed point of \(X\), the key point is to calculate the projective limit, extended to the outlying neighborhoods \(U\) of \(x\)

\[ \tag{5} \varprojlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(U,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i\ne 2N \\ \mathbb{Z}/n & \text{if } i=2N \text{.} \end{cases} \]

As with topological varieties, the case of an open ball must first be treated directly (or simply that of the interval \((0,1)\)), here the case of curves must first be dealt with directly (6.2) The theorem of local acyclicity of smooth morphisms then allows to bring back (5) in this particular case (6.3).

Isomorphisms (4) and (5) are not canonical: they depend on the choice of a orientation \(X\). For \(n\) invertible on a scheme \(X\), \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) is a sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-free modules of rank one. We note \(\mathbb{Z}/n(N)\) its tensor power \(N\)-th (\(N\in\mathbb{Z}\)).5) is

\[ \tag{6} \varprojlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2N} \left(U,\mathbb{Z}/n(N)\right) = \mathbb{Z}/n \text{,} \]

and \(\mathbb{Z}/n(N)\) is called the orientation sheaf of \(X\); the sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}/n(N)\) being constant, isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/n\), it can be removed from the sign \(N\) and written rather

\[ \tag{7} \varprojlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2N} (U,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/n(-N) \text{,} \]

and the duality of Poincaré will take the form of a perfect duality, with values in \(\mathbb{Z}/n(-N)\), between \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2N-i}(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\).

6.2. The case of curves

6.2.1

Is \(\overline{X}\) a projective and smooth curve on \(k\) algebraically closed, and \(n\) invertible on \(\overline{X}\).3.3.5) provides, for \(\overline{X}\) related, canonical isomorphism

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(\overline{X},\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \operatorname{Pic}(\overline{X})/n\operatorname{Pic}(\overline{X}) \ar[r]^-{\deg}_-{\sim} & \mathbb{Z}/n } \]

Be \(D\) a reduced divisor of \(\overline{X}\) and \(X=\overline{X}\setminus D\)

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j & \overline{X} & D . \ar@{_{(}->}[l]_-i} \]

The exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j_! \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_* \boldsymbol{\mu}_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) and the fact that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{X},i_*\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(D,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)=0\) for \(i>0\) provide isomorphism

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(\overline{X},j_! \boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r]^-\sim & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(\overline{X},\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r]^-\sim & \mathbb{Z}/n . } \]

For \(\overline{X}\) disconnexe, we have the same

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = (\mathbb{Z}/n)^{\pi_0(X)} \]

and we define the trace morphism like the sum

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{tr} : \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n)^{\pi_0(X)} \ar[r]^-\Sigma & \mathbb{Z}/n. } \]
Theorem 6.2.2

The form \(\operatorname{tr}(a\smallsmile b)\) identifies each of the two groups \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2-i}(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) to the dual (valued in \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)) of the other.

Transcending evidence. If \(\overline{X}\) is a projective and smooth curve on a line \(S\), and \(j:X\hookrightarrow \overline{X}\) is the inclusion of the complement of a divisor \(D\) spread over \(S\)< the cohomologies (rep. compact support cohomologies) of the special and generic geometric fibres of \(X/S\) are "the same," i.e. locally constant sheaf fibers on \(S\). This is inferred from the similar facts for \(\overline{X}\) and \(D\), via the exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j_! \mathbb{Z}/n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbb{Z}/n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathbb{Z}/n}_D\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) (for compact support cohomology) and formulae \(j_*\mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n\), \(\mathsf{R}^1 j_* \mathbb{Z}/n \simeq {\mathbb{Z}/n}_D(-1)\), \(\mathsf{R}^i j_*\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) (\(i\geqslant 2\)) (for ordinary cohomology) (5.3.4).

This principle of specialisation brings down (6.2.2) in case \(k\) is characteristic \(0\).5.3.3), this case amounts to that where \(k=\mathbb{C}\). Finally, for \(k=\mathbb{C}\), the groups \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) coincide with the groups of the same name, calculated for the classical topological space \(X_{\textnormal{cl}}\) and, via isomorphism \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_n: x\mapsto \exp\left(\frac{2\pi i x}{n}\right)\), trace morphism is identified as "integration on the fundamental class," so that (6.2.2) results from the duality of Poincaré for \(X_{\textnormal{cl}}\).

6.2.3 Algebraic evidence

For a very economical proof, see (V, §2).

Here's another one, related to the self-duality of the Jacobian.

Let's take over the ratings of (6.2.1) One can assume – and one supposes – that \(X\) is related. \(i=0\) and \(i=2\) being trivial, it is also assumed that \(i=1\). Let's define \(_D\mathbb{G}_m\) later accurate \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} _D\mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*\mathbb{G}_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) (sections of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) Congruents to \(1\mod D\)) The group \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X}, _D\mathbb{G}_m)\) classifies invertible sheaves on \(\overline{X}\) Trivialised on \(D\). This is the group of points of \(\operatorname{Pic}_D(\overline{X})\), an extension of \(\mathbb{Z}\) (the degree) by the group of points of a generalised Jacobian of Rosenlicht (corresponding to the conductor \(1\) at each point of \(D\)) \(\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})\), itself extension of the Abelian variety \(\operatorname{Pic}^0(\overline{X})\) by the torus \(\mathbb{G}_m^D/(\mathbb{G}_m \text{ diagonal})\).

  1. The exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} _D\mathbb{G}_m \xrightarrow{n} _D\mathbb{G}_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) provides isomorphism

    \[ \tag{6.2.3.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n \text{.} \]

  2. The application that to \(x\in X(k)\) associates the class of the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{O}(x)\) on \(\overline{X}\), trivialised by \(1\) on \(D\), comes from a morphism

    \[ f:X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}_D(\overline{X}) \text{.} \]

For the next step, you set a base point \(0\) and place \(f_0(x)=f(x)-f(0)\). For any homomorphism \(v:\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}/n\), i.e. \(\overline{v}\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}),\mathbb{Z}/n\right)\) the image by \(v\) of the class in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}),\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n\right)\) of the torsor defined by the extension

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}) \ar[r]^-n & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}) \ar[r] & 0 } \]

The theory of the field of geometric classes (as explained in Serre) [33]) shows that the application \(v\mapsto f_0^*(\overline{v})\):

\[ \tag{6.2.3.2} \hom\left(\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n,\mathbb{Z}/n\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \]

is isomorphism. To deduce (6.2.2) of (6.2.3.1) and (6.2.3.2), it remains to be seen that

\[ \tag{6.2.2.3} \operatorname{tr}\left(u \smallsmile f_0^*(\overline{v})\right) = -v(u) \text{.} \]

6.3 The general case

Be \(X\) a smooth and purely dimensional algebraic variety \(N\), on \(k\) algebraically closed. To state the theorem of duality of Poincaré, we must first define trace morphism

\[ \operatorname{tr} : \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2N}\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(N)\right) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}/n\text{.} \]

The definition is a painful loosening from the hard case of curves [2]. Then we have

Theorem 6.3.1

The form \(\operatorname{tr}(a\smallsmile b)\) identifies each of the groups \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(N)\right)\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2 N-i}(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) to the dual of the other.

Be \(x\in X\) a closed point and \(X_x\) the strict localization of \(X\) in \(x\). We set up, for \(U\) the open neighborhoods of \(x\)

\[ \tag{6.3.1.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X_x,\mathbb{Z}/n) = \varprojlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(U,\mathbb{Z}/n) \text{.} \]

It would be preferable to consider rather the pro-object \(\text{``}\varprojlim\text{''} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(U,\mathbb{Z}/n)\), but as the groups at stake are finished, the difference is not essential. As we have tried to explain in the introduction, (6.3.1) is the result of

\[ \tag{6.3.1.2} \begin{aligned} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(X_x,\mathbb{Z}/n) &= 0 \text{ for } i\ne 2N \text{ and that } \\ \operatorname{tr} : \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{2 N}\left(X_x, \mathbb{Z}/n(N)\right) &\xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbb{Z}/n \text{ is isomorphism.} \end{aligned} \]

The case where \(N=0\) is trivial. If \(N>0\), either \(Y_y\) the strict localized in a closed point of a diagram \(Y\) the pure smooth dimension \(N-1\), and either \(f:X_x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_y\) the essentially smooth morphism (of relative dimension one). The demonstration uses the spectral sequence of Leray in compact support cohomology for \(f\), to return to the case of curves. "compact support cohomology" considered as limits (6.3.1.1), the existence of such a spectral sequence poses various problems of passage to the limit, dealt with with with too many details in [2]. For any geometric point \(z\) of \(Y_y\), we have

\[ (\mathsf{R}^if_! \mathbb{Z}/n)_z = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(f^{-1}(z),\mathbb{Z}/n) \text{.} \]

The geometric fiber \(f^{-1}(z)\) is a projective limit of smooth curves on an algebraically closed field. It checks the duality of Poincaré. Its ordinary cohomology is given by the local acyclic theorem for smooth morphisms:

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(f^{-1}(z),\mathbb{Z}/n) = \begin{cases} \mathbb{Z}/n & \text{for } i=0 \\ 0 & \text{for } i>0 \text{.} \end{cases} \]

By duality, we have

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(f^{-1}(z),\mathbb{Z}/n) = \begin{cases} \mathbb{Z}/n(-1) & \text{for } i=2 \\ 0 & \text{for } i\ne 2 \text{.} \end{cases} \]

and Leray's spectral sequence is written

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i\left(X_x,\mathbb{Z}/n(N)\right) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{i-2}\left(Y_y,\mathbb{Z}/n(N-1)\right) \text{.} \]

We conclude by recurrence on \(N\).

6.4 Variants and Applications

A "formalism of duality" can be constructed in the form of an electic cohomology (\(=\) of the searchers \(\mathsf{R} f_!\), \(\mathsf{R} f_!\), \(\mathsf{R} f^!\) In this language, the results of the preceding paragraph are transcribed as follows: \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is smooth and purely of relative dimension \(N\), and that \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), with \(k\) algebraically closed, then

\[ \mathsf{R} f^! \mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n [2N](N) \text{.} \]

This result is without hypothesis on \(S\). It admits the

Corollary 6.4.1

If \(f\) is smooth and purely of relative dimension \(N\), and the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i f_! \mathbb{Z}/n\) are locally constant, then the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i f_* \mathbb{Z}/n\) are also locally constant, and

\[ \mathsf{R}^i f_* \mathbb{Z}/n = \underline{\hom}\left(\mathsf{R}^{2 N-i} f_! \mathbb{Z}/n(N),\mathbb{Z}/n\right)\text{.} \]

In particular, under the assumptions of the corollary, the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i f_* \mathbb{Z}/n\) are constructible. Hence, it can be shown that, if \(S\) is of the finite type on the spectrum of a Dedekind field or ring, then, for any finite type morphism \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and any constructible sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\), the sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^i f_* \mathscr{F}\) are constructible (VII, 1.1).

blocks.html: Duality of Poincaré
6.1 Introduction

Either \(X\) an oriented topological variety, purely of size \(N\), and suppose that \(X\) admits a finished open cover \(\mathscr{U}=(U_i)_{1\leqslant i\leqslant K}\), such that the non-open intersections \(U_i\) are homeomorphic to balls. For such a variety, the duality theorem of Poincare can appear as follows:

1. The cohomology of \(X\) is the cohomology of Čech corresponding to the coating \(\mathscr{U}\). This is the cohomology of the complex

    \[ \tag{1} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}^{A_0} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}^{A_1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \]

    where \(A_k=\{(i_0,\dotsc,i_k) : i_0<\cdots<i_k\text{ and } U_{i_0}\cap \cdots \cap U_{i_k} \ne \varnothing\}\).

  2. For \(a\in A_k\), \(a=(i_0,\dotsc,i_k)\), either \(U_a=U_{i_1}\cap \cdots \cap U_{i_k}\) or \(j_a\) the inclusion of \(U_a\) in \(X\). The constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}\) on \(X\) admits the resolution (left)

    \[ \tag{2} \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \displaystyle\bigoplus_{a\in A_1} j_{a!}\mathbb{Z} \ar[r] & \displaystyle\bigoplus_{a\in A_0} j_{a!} \mathbb{Z} \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & & \mathbb{Z} . } \]
